Chapter 5: Major Domains on Earth
Earth's Interacting Domains:Lithosphere:
●Solid portion of Earth comprising its crust and thin layers of soil.●Supports life by providing nutrient elements.●Divided into continents and ocean basins.
Atmosphere:
●Gaseous envelope surrounding Earth, vital for life.●Contains oxygen, nitrogen, and other gases necessary for survival.●Protects Earth from harmful solar radiation.
Hydrosphere:
●Encompasses all water on Earth's surface, including oceans, seas,
rivers, lakes, and groundwater.
●Essential for life processes and climate regulation.●Plays a crucial role in the water cycle.
Major Continents:Asia:
●Largest continent, covering one-third of Earth's area.●Separated from Europe by the Ural Mountains.●Tropic of Cancer passes through it.
Europe:
●Lies west of Asia, surrounded by water bodies.●Arctic Circle crosses through it.
Africa:
●Second-largest continent.●Sahara Desert located in its northern part.
Join: https://t.me/ISSF_UPSC (Touch to Join)
●Surrounded by water from all sides.
●Nile River, world's longest river, flows through it.
North America:
●Third-largest continent, linked to South America by the Isthmus of
Panama.
●Surrounded by three oceans.
South America:
●Mostly in the Southern Hemisphere.●Andes Mountains run through its length.●Home to the Amazon, world's largest river.
Australia:
●Smallest continent, entirely in the Southern Hemisphere.●Surrounded by oceans and seas.
Antarctica:
●Huge continent in the Southern Hemisphere.●South Po

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
Oceans:
●Major component of the hydrosphere, interconnected and constantly
moving.
●Three main movements: waves, tides, and ocean currents.
●Five major oceans: Pacific, Atlantic, Indian, Southern, and Arctic.
Pacific Ocean:
●Largest ocean, covering one-third of Earth's surface.●Mariana Trench, deepest part of Earth, located here.●Almost circular shape, bordered by Asia, Australia, North, and South
America.
Atlantic Ocean:
●Second-largest ocean, 'S' shaped.●Flanked by North and South Americas on the west, Europe and Africa
on the east.
●Highly indented coastline, ideal for harbors and ports.
Indian Ocean:
●Triangular shape, bounded by Asia, Africa, and Australia.●Named after India.
Southern Ocean:
●Surrounds Antarctica, extending northward to 60 degrees south latitude.
Arctic Ocean:
●Located within the Arctic Circle, surrounds North Pole.●Connected to the Pacific Ocean by the Bering Strait.●Bound by northern coasts of North America and Eurasia.
Biosphere:
●Zone where land, water, and air converge, supporting all forms of life.
Join: https://t.me/ISSF_UPSC (Touch to Join)
Interesting Terms and Facts:
●Strait:Narrow water passage connecting two large bodies of water,
such as seas and oceans (e.g., Strait of Malacca, Palk Strait).
●Isthmus:Narrow land strip joining two landmasses (e.g., Isthmus of
Panama).
Chapter 6: Major Landforms on Earth
Landforms and Processes:Landforms on Earth's surface result from both internal and external processes.Internal Processes:
●Upliftment and si

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
●Formed due to volcanic activity.
●Examples:Mt. Kilimanjaro, Mt. Fujiyama.
Plateaus:
●Flat tablelands standing above surrounding areas, both young and old.
Examples include the Deccan Plateau, East African Plateau, and
Western Plateau of Australia.
Importance of Plateaus:
●Major mining areas often found on plateaus (e.g., African Plateau for
gold and diamond mining, Chota Nagpur Plateau in India for iron, coal,and manganese).
●Plateau areas often feature waterfalls due to rivers falling from great
heights(e.g., Hundru Falls in Chota Nagpur Plateau, Jog Falls inKarnataka). Lava plateaus are rich in fertile black soil suitable forcultivation.
Plains:
●Large stretches of flatlands generally not more than 200 meters above
sea level. Formed by rivers depositing eroded material along theircourses and in valleys. Fertile and densely populated regions (e.g.,Gangetic Plain in India, Yangtze Plain in China).
Interesting Points:A hill rises higher than surrounding areas, while a steephill over 600 meters is termed a mountain.

chunks.text:
●Climate and Biodiversity:India exhibits a diverse range of climates,
vegetation, and wildlife, contributing to rich biodiversity. Additionally,
India's linguistic and cultural diversity is notable.
●Bordering Countries:India shares its land boundaries with seven
countries: China, Bhutan, Nepal, Pakistan, Afghanistan to the north ornorthwest, and Bangladesh and Myanmar to the east.
Location:
●India lies in the Northern Hemisphere, divided into northern and
southern parts by the Tropic of Cancer.
●Extends from approximately 8°4'N to 37°6'N latitudes and from 68°7'E to
97°25'E longitudes.
●Significant longitudinal extent of about 29°, leading to differences in local
time. Local time changes by four minutes for every one degree oflongitude.
●Indian Standard Time (IST) is set based on the longitude 82°30'E, with
each degree representing approximately four minutes of time difference.
Physical Division of India:
●Peninsula:Land surrounded by water on three sides.
●Time Zones:Unlike large countries like the USA and Canada, India
does not have multiple time zones despite its extensive east-west
stretch.
●Alluvial Deposits:Fine soils carried and deposited by rivers in their
basins.
●Tributaries:Streams or rivers that contribute water to a main river.
●Sunderban Delta:World's largest delta, triangular in shape.●Corals:Skeletons of marine animals called Polyps, forming coral
islands when stacked.
Join: https://t.me/ISSF_UPSC (Touch to Join)
India's Physical Features:
●Diverse Landforms:

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
Ecosystem:A system whereliving organisms interact with each other and
with the physical and chemical factors of the environment, transferringenergy and materials.
Interesting Points:
●The term "Environment" originates from the French word "Environer,"
meaning "neighbourhood."
●Biotic elements refer to living organisms, while abiotic elements are
non-living, such as land.
●World Environment Day is celebrated annually onJune 5th.
Chapter 2: Inside our EarthDynamic Nature of EarthInterior of the Earth:The Earth's interior comprises the Crust, Mantle, and
Core.
●Crust:
○Uppermost layer.
○Thinnest layer, approximately 35 km on continental masses and 5
km on ocean floors.
○Continental crust: Rich in Silica and Alumina (SiAl). Oceanic crust:
Dominated by Silica and Magnesium (SiMa).
●Mantle:Extends up to 2900 km below the crust.●Core:Innermost layer with a radius of about 3500 km. Mainly composed
of Nickel and Iron (NiFe), with high temperature and pressure in the
central core.
Rocks and Minerals:
●Rocks:Natural masses of mineral matter constituting the Earth's crust.
●Types of Rocks:
1.Igneous Rocks:
■Formed from cooling magma. Divided into Intrusive (formed
beneath the surface) and Extrusive (formed on the surface).
Join: https://t.me/ISSF_UPSC (Touch to Join)
■Extrusive igneous rocks, like Basalt, cool rapidly with
fine-grained structures.
■Intrusive igneous rocks, like Granite, cool slowly with large
grains.
2.Sedimentary Rocks:
■Formed from transported and deposited fragments o

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
Key Points:
●Rocks undergo transformation through various geological processes,
forming different types of rocks over time.
●The rock cycle involves three main types of rocks: igneous, sedimentary,
and metamorphic.
●Igneous rocks are formed from cooling magma or lava, either beneath
the Earth's surface (intrusive) or on the surface (extrusive).
●Sedimentary rocks form from the accumulation and compression of
sediments, often transported by wind, water, or ice.
●Metamorphic rocks result from the alteration of existing rocks under high
heat and pressure.
●The rock cycle illustrates how rocks can transition from one type to
another over millions of years.
Interesting Points:
●The deepest mine in the world, located in South Africa, extends
approximately 4 km deep, indicating the depth of Earth's geological
features.
●The Earth's crust constitutes only 1% of its volume, while the mantle
makes up 84%, and the core accounts for 15%
Join: https://t.me/ISSF_UPSC (Touch to Join)
Chapter 3: OUR CHANGING EARTH
Lithospheric Plates and Earth MovementsThe lithosphere consists of plates known as Lithospheric plates, which move
slowly due to the circulation of molten magma within the Earth.
These plate movements result in changes on the Earth's surface, driven by
various forces acting on the plates.
Forces Inside the Earth:
●Endogenic Forces:Operate within the Earth's interior.
●Exogenic Forces:Act on the Earth's surface.
Volcano:A volcano is a vent in the Earth's crust through which molten
m

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
Major Landforms and Evolution:Landforms on Earth's surface evolve
through weathering and erosion processes.
●Weathering:Breaks down rocks on the Earth's surface.
●Erosion:Wears away landscapes by agents such as water, wind, and
ice.
Landforms Formed by Rivers:
1.Meanders:Large bends formed by the twisting and turning of rivers as
they enter plains.
2.Ox-bow Lake:When a meander loop cuts off from the main river, it
forms a curved lake known as an ox-bow lake.
3.Floodplains:River overflow deposits fine soil and sediments along its
banks, creating flat, fertile floodplains.
4.Levees:Raised banks formed by deposited sediments along
riverbanks.
5.Distributaries:Near the sea, the river splits into several streams known
as distributaries, aiding in delta formation.
Coastal Landforms:Erosion and deposition by sea waves shape coastal
features.
1.Sea Caves:Hollow caves formed on rocks by wave erosion.
2.Sea Arches:Enlarged caves leave only the roof, forming sea arches.
Join: https://t.me/ISSF_UPSC (Touch to Join)

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
3.Stacks:Continued erosion may leave behind isolated wall-like
structures called stacks.
4.Sea Cliffs:Steep rocky coasts rising vertically from the sea surface.
Landforms Formed by Ice:
1.Glaciers:Massive bodies of ice that erode landscapes by bulldozing
soil and stones, exposing solid rock beneath.
2.Glacial Hollows:Glaciers carve deep hollows which may fill with water
upon melting, forming lakes.
3.Glacial Moraines:Deposits of material carried by glaciers, including
rocks, sand, and silt, which form moraines upon deposition.
Landforms Formed by Winds:Wind plays a significant role in erosion and
deposition, particularly in deserts.
1.Mushroom Rocks:Rock formations resembling mushrooms found in
deserts.
2.Sand Dunes:Hills-like structures formed by wind-deposited sand.
3.Loess:Large deposits of wind-deposited sand found in areas like
China.
Interesting Points:
●Lithospheric Plates:Earth's crust consists of rigid plates carrying
continents and ocean floors.
●Earthquake Waves:Three types: P waves (longitudinal), S waves
(transverse), and L waves (surface).
●Seismograph:Machine used to measure earthquakes.●Richter Scale:Measures earthquake magnitude.●Waterfalls:Angel Falls (Venezuela), Niagara Falls (Canada-USA
border), Victoria Falls (Zambia-Zimbabwe border) are notable examples.
●Angel Falls is the highest waterfall globally.
Join: https://t.me/ISSF_UPSC (Touch to Join)
Chapter 4: Atmosphere: Our Protective Blanket
Figure: Composition of Air
The atmosphere, a vast blanket of ai

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
Stratosphere:Extends up to 50 km above the Earth's surface, nearly
devoid of clouds, ideal for airplane flight, and contains an ozone layer.
Mesosphere:Third layer extending up to 80 km, where meteorites burn
upon entry from space.
Thermosphere:Temperature increases rapidly with height, containing
the Ionosphere (80-400 km), critical for radio transmission.
Exosphere:Uppermost layer with very thin air, where light gases like
helium and hydrogen float into space.
Weather and Climate:
●Weather:Refers to the short-term conditions of the atmosphere,
fluctuating from hour to hour or day to day.
●Climate:Represents the average weather conditions of a place over a
longer period, providing a more stable outlook.
Temperature:
●The degree of hotness or coldness of the air. Influenced by insolation
(incoming solar energy intercepted by the Earth),decreasing from theEquator to the Poles.
Join: https://t.me/ISSF_UPSC (Touch to Join)

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
Air Pressure:
●Pressure exerted by the weight of air on the Earth's surface.
●Decreases rapidly with altitude, highest at sea level.●Horizontal distribution influenced by air temperature.
○High temperature leads to rising air, creating low-pressure areas
associated with cloudy and wet weather.
○Low temperature results in heavy, cold air, leading to
high-pressure areas associated with clear skies.
Wind:
●Movement of air from high-pressure areas to low-pressure areas.●Three types of winds:
○Permanent Winds:Blow constantly throughout the year, such as
Trade winds, westerlies, and easterlies.
○Seasonal Winds:Change path with the Sun's movement, like
monsoon winds.
○Local Winds:Blow during specific times or seasons, such as sea
breeze, land breeze, and Loo.
Join: https://t.me/ISSF_UPSC (Touch to Join)

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
Moisture in the Air:
●Humidity:Moisture content in the air at any given time. A humid day
indicates high water vapor content. Warmer air has higher capacity to
hold water vapor, increasing humidity.
Formation of Precipitation:
●Evaporation:Water from land and water bodies evaporates into water
vapor.
●Condensation:As water vapor rises and cools, it condenses into water
droplets.
●Precipitation:Condensed water droplets fall to Earth in liquid form,
known as rain.
Types of Rainfall
Interesting Points:
●Air Circulation:Heating causes air to expand and rise, while cooling
results in denser, sinking air. This phenomenon, known as air circulation,influences weather patterns.
●Temperature Measurement:The Celsius scale, invented by Anders
Celsius, measures temperature. Water freezes at 0°C and boils at100°C on this scale.
●Moon's Atmosphere:Absence of air on the moon leads to no air
pressure. Astronauts require protective space suits filled with air toprevent bodily harm due to lack of pressure.
Join: https://t.me/ISSF_UPSC (Touch to Join)

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
Chapter 5: Water
Water Cycle:The water cycle is a continuous process in which water changes
its form and circulates between oceans, atmosphere, and land.
Distribution of Water Bodies:
Ocean Circulation:
●Waves:Alternating rise and fall of ocean water on the surface, often
triggered by seismic events like earthquakes or volcanic eruptions.
Tsunamis, massive tidal waves, can result from such events, causingsignificant damage to coastal areas.
○Example:The 2004 tsunami devastated coastal areas of India,
submerging locations like Indira Point in the Andaman andNicobar Islands.
●Tides:Rhythmic rise and fall of ocean water, influenced by the
gravitational pull of the Sun and Moon.
○Spring Tides:Highest tides occurring during full moon and new
moon phases when the Sun, Moon, and Earth are aligned.
○Neap Tides:Lower tides during the Moon's first and last quarter
phases due to the diagonal gravitational pull of the Sun andMoon.
Join: https://t.me/ISSF_UPSC (Touch to Join)

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
Benefits of High Tides:
●High tides aid navigation by raising water levels near shores, facilitating
easier ship arrival at harbors.
●Fishing benefits from high tides as more fish migrate closer to the shore
during these periods.
●High tides' rise and fall are harnessed to generate electricity in some
regions.
Ocean Currents:
●Streams of water flowing constantly on the ocean surface in specific
directions, categorized as warm or cold.
Join: https://t.me/ISSF_UPSC (Touch to Join)

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
●Warm Currents:Originate near the Equator, moving towards the Poles.
●Cold Currents:Carry water from polar or higher latitudes to tropical or
lower latitudes.
●Ocean currents influence regional temperature conditions and create
ideal fishing grounds where warm and cold currents meet, such as the
seas around Japan and the eastern coast of North America.
Interesting Points:
●Terrarium:An artificial enclosure for small house plants.
●Salinity:The amount of salt in grams present in 1000 grams of water.
Average ocean salinity is 35 parts per thousand.
●Dead Sea:Located in Israel, with a salinity of 340 grams per liter,
allowing swimmers to float due to increased salt density.
●World Water Day:Celebrated on March 22.●Waves:Formed by winds scraping across the ocean surface; stronger
winds create larger waves.
●Tsunami:Japanese term meaning "Harbor waves," often causing
destruction in coastal areas.

chunks.text:
Chapter 6: Natural Vegetation and Wildlife
Relationship Between Land Height and Vegetation:The character of
vegetation changes with altitude due to shifts in climate, impacting naturalvegetation.
Factors such as temperature, moisture, slope, and soil thickness influence
vegetation growth.
Classification of Natural Vegetation:
1.Forests:Flourish in regions with abundant temperature and rainfall,
categorized into dense and open forests.
2.Grasslands:Thrive in areas with moderate rainfall.3.Shrubs:Found in dry regions, comprising thorny shrubs and scrubs.
Forests:
●Tropical Evergreen Forests:
○Also known as tropical rainforests, found near the Equator and
tropics.
○Hot regions with heavy year-round rainfall, leading to evergreen
trees.
○Dense canopies block sunlight penetration, featuring hardwood
trees like rosewood, ebony, and mahogany.
●Tropical Deciduous Forests:
○Found in monsoon regions like India, northern Australia, and
Central America.
○Experience seasonal changes with tree shedding leaves in the dry
season.
○Hardwood trees such as sal, teak, neem, and shisham are
prominent, utilized for various purposes like furniture and
construction.

Temperate Evergreen Forests:
●Located in mid-latitudinal coastal regions, notably on the eastern
margins of continents like southeast USA, South China, and southeast
Brazil.
●Comprise both hard and softwood trees such as oak, pine, and
eucalyptus.
Temperate Deciduous Forests:
●Found in higher

Chapter 4: Agriculture
●The science and art of cultivation on soil, involving raising crops and
rearing livestock. Also known as farming, it's a primary economic
activity.
Types of Economic Activities:
●Primary Activities:
○Connected with the extraction and production of natural
resources.
○Examples include agriculture, fishing, and gathering.
●Secondary Activities:
○Involve the processing of natural resources.
○Examples include manufacturing steel, baking bread, and
weaving cloth.
●Tertiary Activities:
○Provide support to primary and secondary sectors through
services.
○Examples include transport, trade, banking, insurance, and
advertising.

chunks.text:
●Economically Active Group:This group lies between the dependent
populations and represents the working-age population.
General Patterns in Population Pyramids:
●In countries with high birth and death rates, the pyramid is broad at the
base and narrows rapidly towards the top. Example: Kenya.
●Decreasing death rates, especially among infants, result in broader
bases in countries like India, indicating more surviving infants.
●Low birth rates, as seen in Japan, lead to pyramids narrowing at the
base and an increase in the elderly population due to decreased death
rates.
Interesting Points:
●The Ministry of Human Resource Development in India was established
in 1985 to enhance people's skills and capabilities.
●Life expectancy measures the average number of years a person can
expect to live.
●The Pradhan Mantri Kaushal Vikas Yojana (PKVY), initiated in 2015,
aims to provide quality training to one crore Indian youth from 2016 to2020 to develop employable skills.

Major Physiographic Divisions:
1. The Himalayan Mountains:
○Geologicallyyoung and structurally fold mountainsalong

kilometres.
○Width varies from 400 kilometres in Kashmir to 150 kilometres in

Western half.
○Among theloftiest and most rugged mountain barriers
globally.

○Northernmost range.
○Most continuous range with the loftiest peaks, averaging 6,000
meters in height.

3. Himachal or Lesser Himalayas:
○Lies to the South of the Himadri, comprising the most rugged
mountain system.
○Mainly composed of highly compressed and altered rocks.○Altitude varies between 3,700 and 4,500 meters, with an average
width of 50 kilometres.
○Includes prominent ranges likePir Panjal, Dhaula Dhar, and
Mahabharat.

from the main Himalayan ranges.

east.

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
Climate of India:
●The climate of India is described as the 'Monsoon' type, characterized
by seasonal reversal in wind direction during the year.
●This type of climate is mainly found in the south and southeast of Asia.
The Indian Monsoon:
●First noticed by sailors who came to India in historic times and benefited
from the reversal of wind system. Named 'monsoon' by Arab traders.
●Experienced in the tropical area roughly between 20° N and 20° S.
●Duration is typically between 100-120 days from early June to
mid-September. Factors affecting the Mechanism of Monsoon:
●Differential heating and cooling of land and water create low pressure on
land in India and relatively high pressure on seas.
●Shift of the position of Inter Tropical Convergence Zone (ITCZ) in
summer, over the Ganga plain.
●Presence of high-pressure area east of Madagascar, approximately at
20°S over the Indian Ocean.
●Intense heating of the Tibetan plateau during summer, resulting in
strong vertical air currents and low pressure over the plateau.
●Movement of westerly jet stream to the north of the Himalayas and
presence of tropical easterly jet stream over the Indian peninsula during
summer.
●Monsoon is also affected by pressure conditions over the southern
oceans, including the Southern Oscillation (SO). El Niño SouthernOscillations (ENSO):
○Relates to warm ocean current flowing past the Peruvian Coast
every 2 to 5 years, impacting pressure conditions.
○Changes in pressure conditions are connected to El Niño,

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
●It withdraws completely from the northern half of the peninsula by
mid-October.
●The withdrawal from the southern half of the peninsula is relatively rapid.
●By early December, the monsoon mostly withdraws from the rest of
India.
The Seasons:
●The monsoon climate in India exhibits a distinct seasonal pattern,
especially in the interior regions.
●Coastal areas experience less temperature variation, but may have
varying rainfall patterns.
●India experiences four main seasons: the cold weather season (winter),
the hot weather season (summer), the advancing monsoon, and the
retreating monsoon, with some regional variations.
The Cold Weather Season (Winter):
●Duration: Typically begins from mid-November in northern India and
lasts until February, with December and January being the coldestmonths in the north.
●Temperature: Decreases from south to north, with warm days and cold
nights. Frost is common in the north, and higher Himalayan slopesexperience snowfall.
●Wind Patterns: Northeast trade winds prevail over India, blowing from
land to sea, resulting in a dry season for most parts of the country.However, some rainfall may occur on the Tamil Nadu coast as windsblow from sea to land.
●Weather Conditions: Characterized by clear skies, low temperatures,
low humidity, and feeble, variable winds.
●Cyclonic Disturbances: Low-pressure systems, called cyclonic
disturbances, move into India from the west and northwest, originatingover the Mediterranean Sea and Western Asia.
●Importance

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:

○Elephants:Found in hot wet forests of Assam, Karnataka, and

4. Development of Resources
●Resources are essential for survival and quality of life but indiscriminate
use leads to depletion and ecological crises.
●Accumulation of resources in few hands divides society.
●Exploitation causes ecological crises like global warming and pollution.
5. Resource Planning
●Essential for sustainable development and involves identification,
inventory, and matching with national plans.
●Balanced planning necessary due to regional resource disparities.●India's resource planning includes inventory, planning structures, and
integration with national development plans.
6. Conservation of Resources
●Over-utilization leads to socio-economic and environmental problems.●Conservation strategies crucial at various levels.
7. Land Resources
●Vital for supporting natural ecosystems, human life, and economic
activities.
●Land use pattern determined by physical (topography, climate, soil) and
human factors (population density, technology, culture).

Historical Milestones in Environmental Conservation
Brundtland Commission Report, 1987:
●Introduced the concept of 'Sustainable Development' advocating it for
resource conservation.
●Published as a book titled "Our Common Future."
Rio de Janeiro Earth Summit, 1992:
●First International Earth Summit convened to address urgent
environmental and socioeconomic issues globally.
●Leaders signed the Declaration on Global Climatic Change and
Biological Diversity.
●Rio Convention endorsed global Forest Principles and adopted Agenda
21 for achieving Sustainable Development in the 21st century.
Agenda 21:
●Declaration signed by world leaders in 1992 at the United Nations
Conference on Environment and Development (UNCED) in Rio de
Janeiro, Brazil.
●Aims to achieve global sustainable development.
Role of Forests in Ecological Systems:Forests are primary producerscrucial for ecological balance, sustaining diverse life forms.

The classification of roads in India according to their capacity is as
follows:
1.Golden Quadrilateral Super Highways:These are six-lane
superhighways connecting major cities in India. The project aims toreduce travel time and distance between Delhi, Kolkata, Chennai, andMumbai.
a. It includes the North-South corridors linking Srinagar and
Kaniyakumari, as well as the East-West Corridor connectingSilchar and Porbandar.
b. TheNational Highway Authority of India (NHAI)is responsible
for implementing this project.
2.National Highways:National highways connect extreme parts of India
and serve as primary road systems. They are maintained by the CentralPublic Works Department (CPWD).
a. Examples include National Highway No. 1, which runs between
Delhi and Amritsar.
3.State Highways:These roads link state capitals with different district
headquarters. They are constructed and maintained by the State PublicWorks Department (PWD) in states and union territories.
4.District Roads:District roads connect district headquarters with other
places within the district. They are maintained by the Zila Parishad.
5.Other Roads:This category includes rural roads, which connect rural
areas and villages with towns. These roads have received specialattention under schemes like the Pradhan Mantri Grameen SadakYojana, which aims to connect every village in the country to a majortown with an all-season motorable road.

Chapter 1: India - Location
Geographical Extent of India:
●India spans from Kashmir in the north to Kanyakumari in the south, and
from Arunachal Pradesh in the east to Gujarat in the west.
●Territorial limit extends up to 12 nautical miles (about 21.9 km) from the
coast.
●Southern boundary extends up to 6°45' N latitude in the Bay of Bengal.
Location and Dimensions:
●Longitudinal extent: About 30 degrees.
●Latitudinal extent: North-South distance of 3214 km, East-West
distance of 2933 km.
●Total area: 3.28 million sq. km, making up 2.4% of the world's land
surface area.
●India is the seventh-largest country globally.
Factors Influencing Variations:
●Difference between North-South and East-West extent: Longitudinal
distance decreases towards poles, while latitudinal distance remains
constant.
●Southern part within tropics, northern part in sub-tropical or warm
temperate zone.
●These variations influence landforms, climate, soil types, and natural
vegetation.
Impact on People:
●Longitudinal difference: Nearly 30 degrees.
●Two-hour time difference between easternmost and westernmost parts
of India.
●Sunrise occurs two hours earlier in the east compared to the west.
Physical Diversity:
●India's size contributes to its diverse geography, including lofty
mountains in the north, major rivers like the Ganga, Brahmaputra, and
others, green forested hills in the northeast and south, and the vastdesert of Marusthali.
Indian Subcontinent:
●The Indian subcontinent comprises India along wi

●The Indo-Ganga-Brahmaputra Plain
The Peninsular Block:
●Northern boundary: An irregular line from Kachchh, along the western
flank of the Aravali Range, to Delhi, and then roughly parallel to the
Yamuna and the Ganga up to the Raajmahal Hills and the Ganga delta.
●Extensions:
○North Eastern extent: Karbi Anglong and Meghalaya plateau
○Western Extent: Rajasthan
●The North-Eastern parts are separated by the Malda fault in West
Bengal from the Chotanagpur plateau.
●In Rajasthan, desert and other desert-like features overlay this block.●A significant portion of the peninsular region comprises complex and
ancient gneisses and granite formations.
Peninsular Block:
●Since the Cambrian period, the peninsular part has remained a rigid
block, with the exception of the western coast submerged beneath the
sea.
●However, other parts have experienced changes due to tectonic
activity.
●As part of the Indo-Australian plate, the peninsular block undergoes
various vertical movements and block faulting, evident in features likethe rift valleys of Narmada, Tapi, and Mahanadi, and the blockmountain of Satpura.
●The Peninsula mainly comprises relict and residual mountains such as
the Aravali hills, Nallamala hills, Javadi hills, Veliconda hills, Palkondarange, and Mahendragiri hills, among others. River valleys are shallowwith low gradients.
●Most rivers flow towards the east and form deltas, like the Krishna,
Godavari, and Mahanadi.
Himalayas and Other Peninsular Mountains:
●The Himalayas, along

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
Himalayan Drainage System:
●Evolution:
○Developed over an extensive geological timeline.
○Major basins include the Ganga, Indus, and Brahmaputra,
originating from deep gorges formed by concurrent erosional
activity during Himalayan uplift.
●Geological Features:
○Rivers carve out deep gorges, V-shaped valleys, rapids, and
waterfalls in mountainous regions.
○In plains, depositional features like flat valleys, ox-bow lakes,
floodplains, braided channels, and deltas are common near rivermouths.
●River Characteristics:
○Rivers exhibit highly tortuous courses in Himalayan regions,
while displaying strong meandering tendencies in plains, oftencausing frequent course shifts.
○The RiverKosi,known as the 'sorrow of Bihar,' frequently
changes its course due to heavy sediment deposition.
●Evolutionary History:
○During the Miocene period (5-24 million years ago), theShiwalik
or Indo-Brahmariver flowed from Assam to Punjab, eventuallydischarging into the Gulf of Sind near lower Punjab.
○Pleistocene upheavals led to the dismemberment of the
Indo-Brahma river into three main drainage systems:
■The Indus and its tributaries in the western part.
■The Ganga and its Himalayan tributaries in the central part.■The Brahmaputra in Assam and its Himalayan tributaries in
the eastern part.
○Geological events such as the uplift of thePotawar plateau
(Delhi ridge) and the down thrusting of the Malda gap redirected
the flow of the Ganga and Brahmaputra systems towards the Bay
of Bengal.

chunks.text:
Detailed Insights into Jet Streams' Role
●Dynamic Nature: Jet streams exhibit variability in their speed,
direction, and altitude, influenced by factors such as the Earth's rotation
and the distribution of land and sea heating.
●Seasonal Variations: The position and strength of jet streams can
change with the seasons, impacting weather patterns globally andwithin India. For example, the shift of the jet streams can influence thetiming and intensity of the monsoon.
●Interaction with Weather Systems:
○Jet streams can steer cyclonic disturbances, including those
influencing India's weather, especially during the winter andpre-monsoon seasons.
○The interplay between jet streams and other atmospheric
phenomena, such as western disturbances, can lead tosignificant weather events, including heavy rainfall and coldwaves.
Western Cyclonic Disturbance and Tropical Cyclones
Western Cyclonic Disturbances
●Origin and Path: These disturbances originate over the Mediterranean
Sea and enter the Indian subcontinent from the west and northwestduring winter, carried by the westerly jet stream.
●Temperature Indicator: A rise in night temperatures often signals the
approach of these cyclonic disturbances.
●Impact: Although less intense than tropical cyclones, they can cause
significant weather changes, including rain and snow in northern India,which is vital for winter crops.
Tropical Cyclones
●Origin: Arise over the Bay of Bengal and the Indian Ocean,
characterized by high wind velocity and heav

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
●These breaks can significantly impact agricultural activities and water
availability, making their prediction and understanding crucial for
effective monsoon management.
Two Main Branches of the Monsoon
●Arabian Sea Branch:
○First Branch: Encounters the Western Ghats, leading to heavy
rainfall on the windward side, while the leeward side experiencesdrier conditions due to the rain shadow effect.
○Second Branch: Moves inland, affecting central India and
merging with the Bay of Bengal branch in the Ganga plains,facilitating widespread rainfall.
○Third Branch: Affects the Saurashtra Peninsula and Kachchh,
moving towards west Rajasthan and finally joining the Bay ofBengal branch in Punjab and Haryana, influencing rainfall in thewestern Himalayas.
●Bay of Bengal Branch:
○Initial Direction: Strikes the coast of Myanmar and southeast
Bangladesh, with the Arakan Hills deflecting a significant portiontowards the Indian subcontinent.
○Split and Distribution: Splits under the Himalayas' influence,
with one branch moving westward along the Ganga plains andanother ascending the Brahmaputra valley, causing widespreadrainfall.
○Meghalaya Rains: A sub-branch impacts the Garo and Khasi
hills, with Mawsynram on the Khasi hills receiving the highestaverage annual rainfall globally.
Regional Rainfall Variations
●Tamil Nadu's Dry Season: The Tamil Nadu coast remains dry during
this season due to its parallel orientation to the Bay of Bengal branchand its positioning in the rain-shadow area of th

Köppen's climatic classification system
Köppen's climatic classification system, which is widely used to categorize the
world's climates based on temperature, precipitation, and their seasonalpatterns, divides India into various climatic regions reflecting its diverseclimatic conditions.
Climatic Regions of India According to Köppen's Scheme
●Amw: Monsoon with Short Dry Season
○Location: West coast of India, south of Goa.

season with a brief dry period, receiving heavy rainfall, especially
during the monsoon months.

○Location: Coromandal coast of Tamil Nadu.
○Characteristics: Experiences monsoon rains with a dry summer
season, reflecting the influence of the northeast monsoon.
●Aw: Tropical Savannah
○Location: Most of the peninsular plateau and areas south of the
Tropic of Cancer.
○Characteristics: Marked by distinct wet and dry seasons, with
most rainfall occurring during the monsoon period.
●BShw: Semiarid Steppe Climate
○Location: Northwestern Gujarat, parts of western Rajasthan, and
Punjab.
○Characteristics: Receives relatively low rainfall, leading to
semiarid conditions suitable for extensive pastoralism and some
agriculture.
●BWhw: Hot Desert
○Location: Extreme western Rajasthan.
○Characteristics: Characterized by very low precipitation, making
it one of the driest regions in India.
●Cwg: Monsoon with Dry Winter
○Location: Ganga plain, eastern Rajasthan, northern Madhya
Pradesh, and most of Northeast India.
○Characteristics: Experiences a dry winter season followed by a
wet monsoon season, with significant variation in rainfall
distribution throughout the year.
●Dfc: Cold Humid Winter with Short Summer
○Location: Arunachal Pradesh.

○Location: Jammu and Kashmir, Himachal Pradesh,
