chunks.text:
2.Western Ghats and Andaman Nicobar Islands:
○Abundant in tropical rainforests.
3.Deltaic Regions:
○Dominated by tropical forests and mangroves.
4.Desert and Semi-Desert Areas (Rajasthan):
○Known for cacti, various bushes, and thorny vegetation.
Classification of Forests in India:
Based on predominant vegetation types and climatic regions, Indian forests
can be classified into the following groups:
1.Tropical Evergreen and Semi-Evergreen Forests:
○Characterized by dense, lush foliage throughout the year, found
mainly in regions with high rainfall.
2.Tropical Deciduous Forests:
○Marked by the shedding of leaves during a particular season,
prevalent in areas with both high and moderate rainfall.
3.Tropical Thorn Forests:
○Notable for their abundance of thorny vegetation, typically found
in regions with low rainfall.
4.Montane Forests:
○Thrive in mountainous regions, characterized by a variety of
vegetation zones based on altitude.
5.Littoral and Swamp Forests:
○Found in coastal areas and wetlands, characterized by specific
adaptations to the saline environment.
Tropical Evergreen Forest:
●Regions:
○Found in the western slope of the Western Ghats, hills of the
northeastern region, and the Andaman and Nicobar Islands.
●Climatic Conditions:
○Thrive in warm and humid areas with an annual precipitation
exceeding 200 cm and mean annual temperature above 22°C.
●Characteristics:
○Well-stratified with layers from ground shrubs and creepers to
short, structured trees and tall varieties.

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
●Total Biosphere Reserves:
○There are a total of 11 biosphere reserves recognized under
UNESCO's MAB Programme.
Nilgiri Biosphere Reserve:
●Establishment:
○Established in September 1986, it is the first biosphere reserve.
●Area and Coverage:
○Encompasses the sanctuary complex of Wayanad, Nagarhole,
Bandipore, and Mudumalai.
○Includes the entire forested hill slopes of Nilambur, the Upper
Nilgiri plateau, Silent Valley, and the Siruvani hills.
○Total area: Approximately 5,520 sq. km.
●Vegetation:
○Encompasses unspoiled areas of various natural vegetation
types, including dry scrubs, dry and moist deciduous,
semi-evergreen and wet evergreen forests, evergreen sholas,grasslands, and swamps.
●Fauna:
○Houses the largest known population of two endangered animal
species: the Nilgiri Tahr and the Lion-tailed macaque.
○Hosts the largest south Indian population of elephants, tigers,
gaurs, sambars, and chitals.
○Supports a significant number of endemic and endangered plant
species.
●Topography:
○Exhibits extremely varied topography.
Nanda Devi Biosphere Reserve:
●Location:
○Situated in Uttarakhand, covering parts of Chamoli, Almora,
Pithoragarh, and Bageshwar districts.
●Forest Types:
○Major forest types are temperate.
●Flora:
○Includes silver weed and orchids like latifolie and rhododendron.
●Fauna:
○Rich biodiversity, including species like snow leopard, black bear,
brown bear, musk deer, snowcock, golden eagle, and black
eagle.
●Major Threats:
○Collection of endangered plants for m

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
CHAPTER 6: SOILS
Soil, as the most significant layer of the Earth's crust, serves as a crucial
resource supporting various life forms and agricultural activities. Its formationinvolves the interplay of several factors including weathering, gradation, andhuman activities.
Understanding its composition and layers is vital for effective land
management and agriculture.
Formation of Soils:
●Soil is a mixture of rock debris and organic materials developed on the
Earth's surface.
●Agents of weathering and gradation act upon parent rock materials to
create a thin layer of soil.
●Factors influencing soil formation include relief, parent material,
climate, vegetation, other life forms, and time.
●Human activities significantly influence soil formation.●Soil components include mineral particles, humus, water, and air.
Layers of Soils:Soil is stratified into layers known as horizons, forming the soil profile.1. Horizon A (Topsoil):
●The topmost zone where organic materials mix with mineral matter,
nutrients, and water, essential for plant growth.
●Rich in organic matter.
2. Horizon B (Subsoil):
●Transition zone between Horizon A and Horizon C.●Contains matter derived from both above and below.●Exhibits noticeable weathering of mineral matter, with some organic
content.
3. Horizon C (Parent Material):
●Composed of loose parent material.
●Represents the initial stage of soil formation, eventually contributing to
the formation of Horizon A and B.
4. Bedrock (Parent Rock):
●The rock layer b

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
Alluvial Soils:
●Distribution:
○Widespread in the northern plains and river valleys, covering
approximately 40% of the total area of India.
○Found in deltas of the east coast and river valleys in the
Peninsular region.
○Extend into the plains of Gujarat through a narrow corridor in
Rajasthan.
●Formation:
○Depositional soils transported and deposited by rivers and
streams.
●Variability:
○Vary in nature from sandy loam to clay.
○Generally rich in potash but poor in phosphorus.
●Types in the Upper and Middle Ganga Plain:
○Khadar:New alluvium deposited annually by floods, enriched
with fine silts.
○Bhangar:Older alluvium deposited away from flood plains,
containing calcareous concretions (Kankars).
●Characteristics:
○Alluvial soils in the lower and middle Ganga plain and the
Brahmaputra valley are loamier and more clayey.
○Sand content decreases from west to east.○Colour varies from light grey to ash grey, depending on depth of
deposition, material texture, and maturity.
○Intensively cultivated due to their fertility and suitability for a
variety of crops.
Black Soil:
●Distribution:
○Covers most of the Deccan Plateau, including parts of
Maharashtra, Madhya Pradesh, Gujarat, Andhra Pradesh, and
some parts of Tamil Nadu.
○Found in upper reaches of the Godavari and Krishna rivers and
the northwestern part of the Deccan Plateau.
●Characteristics:
○Also known as 'Regur Soil' or 'Black Cotton Soil'.
○Generally clayey, deep, and impermeable.○Swell and become sticky when wet, shrink when

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
National organizations such as the National Geophysical Laboratory,
Geological Survey of India, Department of Meteorology, and National Instituteof Disaster Management have divided India into five earthquake zones basedon risk levels.
1.Very High Damage Risk Zone:
○Includes the northeastern states, areas to the north of Darbhanga
and Araria along the Indo-Nepal border in Bihar, Uttarakhand,Western Himachal Pradesh (around Dharamshala), KashmirValley in the Himalayan region, and Kutch (Gujarat).
○Notable for experiencing some of the most devastating
earthquakes in India.
2.High Damage Risk Zone:
○Encompasses the remaining parts of Jammu and Kashmir,
Ladakh, Himachal Pradesh, northern parts of Punjab, easternparts of Haryana, Delhi, western Uttar Pradesh, and northernBihar.
3.Moderate Damage Risk Zone:
4.Low Damage Risk Zone:5.Very Low Damage Risk Zone:
The first two zones have witnessed significant seismic activity and have been
prone to devastating earthquakes.
Significant Locations in Each Zone:
●Very High Damage Risk Zone:
○Northeastern states○Areas along the Indo-Nepal border in Bihar○Uttarakhand○Western Himachal Pradesh (around Dharamshala)○Kashmir Valley○Kutch (Gujarat)
●High Damage Risk Zone:
○Remaining parts of Jammu and Kashmir
○Ladakh○Himachal Pradesh○Northern parts of Punjab○Eastern parts of Haryana○Delhi○Western Uttar Pradesh○Northern Bihar
Atmospheric Terrestrial Aquatic Biological
Blizzards Earthquakes Floods Plants and Animals as
colonisers (Locusts, etc.)
Thund

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
Types of Drought:
1.Meteorological Drought:
○Prolonged period of inadequate rainfall with uneven distribution
over time and space.
2.Agricultural Drought:
○Also known as soil moisture drought, characterized by low soil
moisture levels detrimental to crop growth, leading to crop
failures.
○Areas with over 30% of gross cropped area under irrigation are
excluded from this category.
3.Hydrological Drought:
○Occurs when water availability in various storages and reservoirs
like aquifers, lakes, and reservoirs falls below levels replenishedby precipitation.
4.Ecological Drought:
○Results in the failure of natural ecosystem productivity due to
water shortage, leading to ecological distress and ecosystemdamage.
Drought Prone Areas in India:
●Approximately 19% of the country's total geographical area and 12% of
its population suffer from drought annually.
●Around 30% of India's total area, affecting roughly 50 million people, is
identified as drought-prone.
Regional Division based on Severity:
1.Extreme Drought Affected Areas:
○Most parts of Rajasthan, particularly areas west of the Aravali
hills (Marusthali) and Kachchh region of Gujarat.
○Districts like Jaisalmer and Barmer receive less than 90 mm
average annual rainfall.
2.Severe Drought Prone Areas:
○Parts of eastern Rajasthan, most parts of Madhya Pradesh,
eastern parts of Maharashtra, interior parts of Andhra Pradesh,
Karnataka Plateau, northern parts of interior Tamil Nadu,southern parts of Jharkhand, and interior Odisha.
3.Mod

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
Chapter 1: Geography as a Discipline
Branches of Geography Based on Schematic Approach
Physical Geography:
1.Geomorphology:Study of landforms, evolution, and related processes.2.Climatology:Focus on atmosphere structure, weather elements,
climates, and climatic regions.
3.Hydrology:Study of water realms on Earth's surface, including oceans,
lakes, rivers, and their impact on life forms.
4.Soil Geography:Examination of soil formation, types, fertility, distribution,
and utilization.

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
Biogeography:
1.Plant Geography:Examines spatial patterns of natural vegetation.
2.Zoo Geography:Studies spatial patterns and characteristics of animals
and habitats.
3.Ecology/Ecosystem:Scientific study of species' habitat characteristics.4.Environmental Geography:Addresses global environmental issues such
as land degradation, pollution, and conservation.
Interesting Points:
●The term “geography” coined by Greek scholar Eratosthenes, derived
from "geo" (earth) and "graphos" (description).
●Soils formed through pedogenesis process.
Chapter 2: The Origin and the Evolution of the Earth
Modern Theories of Evolution: Origin of the Universe
Big Bang Theory:
●Proposed as the expanding universe hypothesis.●Edwin Hubble's evidence in 1920 showed the universe's expansion.●Distance between galaxies increases, indicating ongoing expansion.
Development of the Universe:
●Initially, all matter existed in a singular atom with infinite temperature and
density.
●Big Bang occurred around 13.7 billion years ago, leading to rapid
expansion.
●Energy converted into matter, and within three minutes, the first atom
formed.
●Temperature dropped, making the universe transparent.
Formation of Stars:
●Galaxies form from accumulation of hydrogen gas in nebulae.●Localized clumps of gas grow denser, forming stars, believed to occur 5-6
billion years ago.
Galaxies:
●Contain numerous stars spread over vast distances, with diameters
ranging from 80,000-150,000 light-years.
●Formation influenced by uneven dist

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
●Jovian Planets:
○Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune.
○Located farther from the star, larger, gaseous, and have thick
atmospheres.
Reasons for Differences in Terrestrial and Jovian Planets:
●Location:Terrestrial planets formed closer to the star, while Jovian
planets formed farther away.
●Solar Winds:Intense solar winds near the star removed gases from
terrestrial planets but not from Jovian planets.
●Size:Terrestrial planets are smaller and have lower gravity, leading to the
loss of escaping gases.
The Moon:
●Earth's only natural satellite.
Theories Related to Origin of the Moon:
●Sir George Darwin's Theory (1838):
○Proposed Earth and Moon formed a single rapidly rotating body,
eventually breaking apart.
○Material forming the moon separated from the depression occupied
by the Pacific Ocean.
●The Big Splat (Giant Impact) Theory:
○A large body collided with Earth shortly after its formation, blasting
material into space.
○The material orbited Earth and coalesced to form the Moon around
4.44 billion years ago.
Evolution of the Earth:
Primordial Earth:
●Initially barren, rocky, and hot with a thin atmosphere of hydrogen and
helium.
●Evolution of life occurred between 4,600 million years ago and the
present.
●Earth has a layered structure, with materials varying in density from the
atmosphere to the core.
Evolution of the Lithosphere:
●Primordial Earth was volatile, with increasing density leading to rising
temperatures.
●Heavier materials like iron sank towards the center, whi

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
Body Waves:
●Generated at the focus and travel through Earth's body.
●P-waves (Primary Waves):
○Fastest, arrive first.○Similar to sound waves, can travel through gaseous, liquid, and
solid materials.
●S-waves (Secondary Waves):
○Arrive after P-waves.○Can only travel through solid materials, aiding in understanding
Earth's interior structure.
Surface Waves:
●Generated when body waves interact with surface rocks.●Move along the surface, last to register on seismograph.

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
●More destructive, cause displacement of rocks and collapse of structures.
Propagation of Earthquake Waves:
●Waves cause vibrations in rocks as they propagate.
●P-waves:
○Vibrate parallel to wave direction, exerting pressure in the direction
of propagation.
○Create density differences, leading to stretching and squeezing of
material.
●S-waves:
○Vibrate perpendicular to wave direction in the vertical plane.○Create troughs and crests in material they pass through.
Emergence of Shadow Waves:
●Shadow Zone:
○Zones where waves are not reported.○Each earthquake has its own distinct shadow zone.
●Observations:
○Seismographs within 105° from the epicenter record both P and
S-waves.
○Seismographs beyond 145° record P-waves but not S-waves.○Zone between 105° and 145° from the epicenter identified as the
shadow zone for both types of waves.
●Shadow Zone Characteristics:
○Beyond 105°, no S-waves are received.○Shadow zone of S-waves larger than that of P-waves, covering over
40% of Earth's surface.
○Shadow zone of P-waves appears as a band around the Earth
between 105° and 145° from the epicentre.
Types of Earthquakes:
●Tectonic Earthquake:
○Most common, caused by sliding of rocks along fault planes.
●Volcanic Earthquake:
○Occur in areas of active volcanoes, a subset of tectonic
earthquakes.
●Collapse Earthquake:
○Result from roofs of underground mines collapsing in intense mining
areas, causing minor tremors.
●Explosion Earthquake:
○Ground shaking due to explosions of chemical or nuclear

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
●Lithosphere:
○Crust and uppermost mantle, thickness ranges from 10-200 km.
●Lower mantle extends beyond asthenosphere, solid in state.
The Core:
●Core-mantle boundary at 2,900 km depth.
●Outer core: liquid state.●Inner core: solid state.●Nife Layer:
○Core primarily composed of nickel and iron, heavy materials.
Volcanoes and Volcanic Landforms:
●Volcano:
○Site where gases, ashes, and/or molten rock (lava) escape to
ground.
●Active Volcano:
○Releases or recently released materials.
●Magma:
○Molten rock material from asthenosphere.
●Lava:
○Magma reaching surface.
●Materials released: lava flows, pyroclastic debris, volcanic bombs, ash,
dust, gases.
Classification of Volcanoes:
Shield Volcanoes:
●Largest volcanoes, primarily basalt flows.
●Not steep due to fluid basalt lava.●Low explosivity, may become explosive with water.●Fountain-like lava movement can form cinder cones.
Composite Volcanoes:
●Erupt cooler, viscous lavas.●Result in explosive eruptions.●Large quantities of pyroclastic material and ashes accumulate around
vent openings, forming layers.
Caldera:
●Most explosive volcanoes.●Tend to collapse on themselves during eruption.

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
●Depressions formed by collapse are calderas.
●Indicate large, close magma chambers.
Flood Basalt Province:
●Characterized by highly fluid lava flowing long distances.●Individual flows can exceed 50 m in thickness and extend for hundreds of
kilometers.
●Example: Deccan Traps covering much of the Maharashtra plateau.
Mid-Oceanic Ridge Volcanoes:
●Found in oceanic areas.●Mid-ocean ridges extend over 70,000 km across all ocean basins,
experiencing frequent eruptions in their central portions.
Volcanic Landforms:
Intrusive Landforms:
●Lava released during volcanic eruptions cools to form igneous rocks.●Cooling can occur either upon reaching the surface or within the crust.

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
●Intrusive forms result from lava cooling within the crust.
Batholiths:
●Large bodies of magmatic material cooling deep in the crust, forming large
domes.
●Surface exposure occurs after denudational processes remove overlying
materials.
●Can cover large areas and extend several kilometers in depth.
●Mostly composed of granite.●Represent the cooled portions of magma chambers.
Lacoliths:
●Large dome-shaped intrusive bodies with level bases, connected by
pipe-like conduits from below.
●Resemble surface volcanic domes but located at deeper depths.●Examples include the domal hills of granite rocks in the Karnataka
plateau.
Lapolith:
●Lava moving upwards may also move horizontally along weak planes,
settling in various forms.
●If it develops into a saucer-shaped body concave towards the sky, it's
termed a lapolith.
Phacolith:
●Wavy masses of intrusive rocks, often found at the base of synclines or
the top of anticlines in folded igneous terrain.
●These wavy materials have a definite conduit to a source beneath, usually
magma chambers subsequently developed as batholiths.
Sills or Sheets:
●Near-horizontal bodies of intrusive igneous rocks.●Thinner ones are referred to as sheets, while thicker horizontal deposits
are called sills.
Dykes:
●Formed when lava solidifies perpendicular to the ground as it moves
through cracks and fissures in the land.
●Cooled in this position, creating a wall-like structure.
These volcanic landforms are commonly found in the western Maharashtra
area and ar

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
●E.g., ancient rocks from Brazil match those from western Africa.
●Earliest marine deposits on South America and Africa suggest recent
separation.
3. Tillite
●Sedimentary rock from glacial deposits.●Gondwana system's tillite found in various Southern Hemisphere
landmasses.
●Indicates past glaciation and continental drift.
Distribution of Oceans and Continents1. Placer Deposits
●Gold placer deposits in Ghana coast with no local source rock.●Gold veins in Brazil suggest deposition during continental proximity.
2. Distribution of Fossils
●Lemurs found in India, Madagascar, and Africa suggest former land
bridge.
●Mesosaurus fossils only in Southern Africa and Brazil, indicating
continental proximity.
Force for DriftingWegener's Explanations:
●Polar-fleeing force: Related to Earth's rotation.●Tidal force: Attraction of moon and sun causing oceanic tides.●Wegener posited long-term effects of these forces.
Post Drift StudiesNew Discoveries:
●Post-World War II discoveries enriched geological knowledge.●Ocean floor mapping revealed insights into ocean and continent
distribution.
Convectional Current Theory
Arthur Holmes' Contribution (1930s):
●Proposed convection currents in mantle due to radioactive elements.●Thermal differences in mantle drive these currents.●Suggested a mantle-wide system of such currents.●Proposed as an alternative to explain forces behind continental drift.
Mapping of the Ocean FloorPost-War Expeditions:
●Detailed mapping of ocean floor revealed:
○Submerged mount

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
Concept of Sea Floor Spreading
●Mapping and Palaeomagnetic Studies:
○Mid-oceanic ridges: Common volcanic eruptions, lava brings
similarities in rocks.
○Age of rocks increases away from crest.
○Oceanic crust rocks younger (not over 200 million years) than
continental rocks (up to 3,200 million years).
○Thin ocean floor sediments (not over 200 million years old).
●Earthquake Distribution:
○Deep trenches: Deep-seated earthquakes.○Mid-oceanic ridges: Shallow earthquake foci.
Hess's Sea Floor Spreading Hypothesis
●Proposed by Hess in 1961.●Constant eruptions at oceanic ridges cause crust rupture, new lava
pushes crust.
●Younger oceanic crust spreading doesn't shrink existing crust, suggesting
crust consumption.
●Pushed ocean floor sinks at trenches, gets consumed.
Plate Tectonics
Introduction
●1967: McKenzie, Parker, and Morgan introduced Plate Tectonics concept.
●Tectonic Plate: Massive, irregularly shaped slab of solid rock, composed
of continental and oceanic lithosphere.
●Plates move horizontally over asthenosphere as rigid units.●Lithosphere: Includes crust and top mantle, thickness varies (5-100 km in
oceanic, about 200 km in continental areas).
Plate Characteristics
●Continental vs. Oceanic Plates:
○Composition based on larger portion of continent or ocean.
●Earth's Lithosphere: Divided into seven major and minor plates.

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
●Surroundings: Young Fold Mountain ridges, trenches, faults around major
plates.
Plate Movement
●Plate, not continent, moves (contrary to Wegener's belief).
●Continents part of plates; Plate movement responsible for continental drift.●Continents wandered throughout geological periods; Pangaea result of
converging plates.
Indian Subcontinent Position
●Indian subcontinent position traced from Nagpur area rock analysis.
Major Plates
1. Antarctica and surrounding oceanic plate.2. North American Plate:
○Includes western Atlantic floor.○Separated from South American plate by Caribbean islands.
3. South American Plate:
○Includes western Atlantic floor.○Separated from North American plate by Caribbean islands.
4. Pacific Plate5. India-Australia-New Zealand Plate6. Africa Plate:
○Includes eastern Atlantic floor.
Minor Plates
1.Cocos Plate:
○Between Central America and Pacific plate.
2.Nazca Plate:
○Between South America and Pacific plate.
3.Arabian Plate:
○Mostly Saudi Arabian landmass.
4.Philippine Plate:
○Between Asiatic and Pacific plate.
5.Caroline Plate:
○Between Philippine and Indian plate (north of New Guinea).
6.Fuji Plate:
○Northeast of Australia.
Types of Plate Boundaries
1. Divergent Boundaries
●New crust generated as plates pull away from each other.
●Sites of plate separation called spreading sites.●Example: Mid-Atlantic Ridge separating American Plate(s) from Eurasian
and African Plates.

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
2. Convergent Boundaries
●Crust destroyed as one plate dives under another.
●Location of sinking plate called subduction zone.●Convergence occurs in three ways:
○Between oceanic and continental plates.○Between two oceanic plates.○Between two continental plates.
3. Transform Boundaries
●Crust neither produced nor destroyed; plates slide horizontally past each
other.
●Transform faults perpendicular to mid-oceanic ridges.●Differential movement and Earth's rotation affect separated plate blocks.
Rates of Plate Movements
●Determined by strips of normal and reverse magnetic field parallel to
mid-oceanic ridges.
●Rates vary considerably:
○Arctic Ridge: Slowest rate (< 2.5 cm/yr).○East Pacific Rise near Easter Island: Fastest rate (> 15 cm/yr).
Force for Plate Movement
●Initially, Earth believed motionless.●Sea floor spreading and plate tectonics theory reveal Earth's dynamic
nature.
●Mobile rock beneath plates moves circularly.●Heat rises, spreads, cools, sinks in convection cells.●Heat sources: Radioactive decay, residual heat.●Arthur Holmes' idea influenced Harry Hess' seafloor spreading concept.●Slow movement of hot mantle below plates drives plate movement.
The Indian Plate
●Includes Peninsular India and Australian continental portions.
●Northern boundary: Subduction zone along Himalayas,
continent-continent convergence.
●Eastern boundary: Extends throughRakinyoma Mountains to Java
Trench island arc.
●Eastern margin: Spreading site east of Australia, SW Pacific oceanic
ridge.
●W

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
○Subduction processes can carry crustal rocks into the mantle,
where they may melt due to increased temperatures, forming
molten magma.
●Key Processes:
○Subduction:Movement of crustal rocks into the Earth's mantle.
○Melting:Transformation of rocks into molten magma due to
increased temperatures.
○Solidification:Cooling and solidification of magma to form igneous
rocks.
○Weathering and Erosion:Breakdown and transportation of rocks
and minerals by natural processes.
○Lithification:Compression and cementation of sediments to form
sedimentary rocks.
○Metamorphism:Transformation of rocks due to changes in
pressure, temperature, or chemical environment.
Interesting Points:
●Petrology:
○Petrology is the scientific study of rocks in all their aspects,
including mineral composition, texture, structure, origin, occurrence,
alteration, and relationship with other rocks.
○Petrologists specialize in analyzing and understanding the
properties and characteristics of rocks to unravel the geologicalhistory and processes shaping the Earth's crust.
Chapter 6: Geomorphic Processes
Definition of Geomorphic Processes:
●Geomorphic processes entail both endogenic and exogenic forces that
exert physical stresses and chemical actions on Earth materials.
●These processes induce changes in the configuration of the Earth's
surface.
Geomorphic Agents:
●Geomorphic agents are exogenic elements of nature capable of acquiring
and transporting Earth materials.
●Examples include running water, glaciers, wind, e

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
Various Exogenic Geomorphic Processes
Weathering:
●Weathering involves the mechanical disintegration and chemical
decomposition of rocks due to various elements of weather and climate.
●It is an in-situ process, occurring without significant motion of materials.●Weathering processes are influenced by geological, climatic, topographic,
and vegetative factors, with climate playing a significant role.
●Weathering can be classified into chemical and physical processes.
Chemical Weathering Processes:
●Chemical processes such as solution, carbonation, hydration, oxidation,
and reduction decompose rocks through chemical reactions involving
oxygen, water, and acids.
●Presence of water, air, and heat accelerates chemical reactions, with
carbon dioxide from decomposition enhancing reactions underground.

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
Physical Weathering Processes:
●Physical or mechanical weathering relies on applied forces such as
gravitational forces (overburden pressure, load, shearing stress),
expansion forces (temperature changes, crystal growth, animal activity),and water pressures (wetting and drying cycles).
●Thermal expansion and pressure release contribute significantly to
physical weathering, causing fatigue in rocks due to repeated contractionand expansion.
Biological Activity and Weathering:
●Biological weathering involves the contribution to or removal of minerals
and ions from the weathering environment by organisms.
●Organisms like earthworms, termites, and rodents aid in exposing new
surfaces to chemical attack through burrowing and wedging.
●Human activities such as vegetation disturbance, soil cultivation, and
ploughing create new contacts between air, water, and minerals,enhancing weathering processes.
●Decaying plant and animal matter produce acids that enhance decay and
solubility of elements, while plant roots mechanically break rocks.
Exfoliation:
●Exfoliation is a result of unloading, thermal contraction and expansion,
and salt weathering rather than a distinct process.
●It can occur due to temperature-induced expansion and contraction.
●Exfoliation domes and tors are examples resulting from unloading and
thermal expansion, respectively.
Significance of Weathering:
●Weathering processes break down rocks into smaller fragments,
facilitating the formation of regolith, soils, erosion,

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
In Western Ghats:
●In contrast to the Himalayas, the Nilgiris bordering Tamil Nadu, Karnataka,
Kerala, and the Western Ghats along the west coast are relatively
tectonically stable.
●They are mostly composed of very hard rocks.
●Despite the stability, debris avalanches and landslides occur in this region.●Many slopes in the Western Ghats and Nilgiris feature steep inclines with
almost vertical cliffs and escarpments.
●Mechanical weathering, primarily induced by temperature changes and
ranges, is significant.
●The region receives heavy rainfall over short periods, which contributes to
the occurrence of landslides and debris avalanches.
Erosion and Deposition:Erosion:
●Erosion involves the acquisition and transportation of rock debris by
various erosional agents, which subsequently deposit the transported
material in plain areas.
●It occurs when massive rocks break into smaller fragments through
weathering.
●Erosional geomorphic agents such as running water, groundwater,
glaciers, wind, and waves remove and transport the debris depending ontheir dynamics.
●Abrasion by rock debris carried by these agents significantly aids in
erosion.
●Weathering, mass wasting, and erosion are degradational processes
responsible for continuous changes in the Earth's surface.
●Denudational processes like erosion and transportation are controlled by
kinetic energy.
●Erosion and transportation of Earth materials are facilitated by wind,
running water, glaciers, waves, and groundwater.
○Wind, runnin

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
●Its influence is determined by the exposure of a surface covered by parent
materials to sunlight and the surface and sub-surface drainage over and
through the parent materials.
●Soils tend to be thinner on steep slopes and thicker over flat upland areas.
●Gentle slopes with slow erosion and good water percolation are conducive
to soil formation.
●Flat areas may develop thick layers of clay with significant accumulation of
organic matter, resulting in dark-coloured soils.
3. Climate:Climate is a significant active factor in soil formation, influencing soil
development through various climatic elements:
4. Moisture:
●Moisture, in terms of its intensity, frequency, and duration of precipitation,
as well as evaporation and humidity, plays a crucial role.
○Precipitation provides soil with moisture necessary for chemical and
biological activities. Excess water aids in the downward
transportation of soil components (eluviation) and their depositionbelow (illuviation).
○In regions with high rainfall, such as wet equatorial areas, significant
removal of calcium, sodium, magnesium, potassium, and silica fromthe soil occurs. The removal of silica is termed desilication.
○In tropical and moderately wet climates, calcium carbonate nodules
(kanker) may form in the soil.
5. Temperature:
●Temperature, considering seasonal and diurnal variations, influences soil
formation.
○It affects chemical and biological activities differently: higher
temperatures increase chemical activity but may reduc

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
●Incised or entrenched meanders are deep and wide meanders cut into
hard rocks, representing a distinct geological feature.
River Terraces:
●River terraces are surfaces representing old valley floors or floodplain
levels. They can be either bedrock surfaces without alluvial cover or
consist of stream deposits known as alluvial terraces.
●These terraces are primarily products of erosion, resulting from vertical
erosion by the stream into its own depositional floodplain.
●Multiple terraces may exist at various heights, indicating former river-bed
levels.
●Paired Terraces: River terraces occurring at the same elevation on both
sides of a river.
Depositional Landforms (Running Water):
Alluvial Fans:
●Formed when streams originating from higher elevations intersect foot
slope plains with low gradients.
●Streams carrying coarse loads from mountain slopes deposit this material
when gradients become too gentle, creating broad, low to high
cone-shaped deposits known as alluvial fans.

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
●Larger materials are deposited initially when a stream channel transitions
into a gentle slope.
●Finer materials like sand, silt, and clay are transported by slower-moving
waters in gentler channels, depositing over the bed and spilling over
banks during flooding.
●Floodplains consist of two types of deposits: flood deposits and channel
deposits.
●In plains, shifting channels occasionally leave cut-off courses that
gradually fill up with coarse deposits.
●Flood deposits from spilled waters contain finer materials like silt and clay.
●Delta floodplains are termed delta plains.
Natural Levees and Point Bars:
●Associated with floodplains, natural levees are low, linear ridges of coarse
deposits along large river banks, often appearing as individual mounds.
●Point bars, or meander bars, are found on the concave side of meanders
in large rivers. They consist of sediments deposited linearly by flowing
waters along the bank, featuring a uniform profile and width andcontaining mixed sediment sizes.
Meanders:
●In expansive flood and delta plains, rivers commonly exhibit meandering,
loop-like channel patterns.
●Meanders are not considered landforms but rather channel patterns
resulting from:
○Water's tendency to laterally erode banks on very gentle gradients.
○Alluvial deposits comprising the banks, which are unconsolidated
and irregular, providing opportunities for lateral pressure exertion by
water.
○The Coriolis force, deflecting fluid water similarly to how it affects
wind.
●Minor

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
●Mechanical removal of materials by moving groundwater is generally
insignificant in forming landforms, making the results of groundwater's
work less apparent in all rock types.
●However, in rocks rich in calcium carbonate, such as limestone or
dolomite, surface water and groundwater, through chemical processes ofsolution and precipitation deposition, create various landforms.
Depositional Landforms by Groundwater:
●Limestone's chief chemical, calcium carbonate, readily dissolves in
carbonated water (rainwater with absorbed carbon dioxide).
●Calcium carbonate deposits when the water carrying it evaporates or
loses its carbon dioxide as it trickles over rough rock surfaces.
Stalactites, Stalagmites, and Pillars:
●Stalactites:Hang like icicles of varying diameters, typically broad at their
bases and tapering towards the free ends, presenting a variety of forms.
●Stalagmites:Rise from the cave floor due to dripping water from the
surface or through the thin pipe of the stalactite immediately below it.They may resemble columns, discs, with smooth, rounded bulging ends,or miniature crater-like depressions.
●Stalagmites and stalactites eventually merge to form columns and
pillars of varying diameters.

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
Karst Topography (Erosional Landforms):
Pools, Sinkholes, Lapies, and Limestone Pavements:
●Swallow holes, small to medium-sized round to sub-rounded shallow
depressions, form on limestone surfaces through solution.
●Sinkhole: Circular at the top and funnel-shaped towards the bottom,
varying in size from a few square meters to a hectare.
●Sinkholes may form solely through solution action (solution sinks) or begin
as solution forms and collapse if the bottom of a sinkhole forms the roof of
an underground void or cave (collapse sinks), also known as dolines.
●Solution sinks are more common than collapse sinks. Surface runoff
enters swallow and sinkholes, flowing as underground streams andre-emerging downstream through cave openings.
●When sinkholes and dolines merge due to material slumping along their
margins or cave roof collapse, they form long, narrow to wide trenchescalled valley sinks or Uvalas.
●Over time, limestone surfaces are eroded into pits, trenches, and irregular
mazes of points, grooves, and ridges known as lapies.
●Limestone Pavements: Lapie fields may eventually become somewhat
smooth.
Caves:
●Cave formation is prominent in areas where there are alternating beds of
rocks (such as shales, sandstones, quartzites) with limestones ordolomites in between, or in regions where limestones are dense, massive,and occur as thick beds.
●Water percolates through materials or cracks and joints, moving
horizontally along bedding planes.
●Caves: Long, narrow to wide gaps forme

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
Horns and Serrated Ridges:
●Horns: High, sharp-pointed, steep-sided peaks formed when three or
more radiating glaciers cut headward until their cirques meet.
●Divides between cirque side walls narrow due to erosion, forming serrated
or saw-toothed ridges, sometimes called arêtes, with sharp crests and a
zigzag outline.
Glacial Valleys/Troughs:
●Glaciated valleys: Trough-like and U-shaped with broad floors and
relatively smooth, steep sides.
●Valleys may contain scattered debris or moraine-shaped debris with a
swampy appearance.
●Hanging valleys may occur at higher elevations on one or both sides of
the main glacial valley.
●Faces of divides or spurs of hanging valleys opening into the main glacial
valley are often truncated, resembling triangular facets.
●Very deep glacial troughs filled with seawater, forming shorelines in high
latitudes, are called fjords/fiords.
Depositional Landforms Formed by Glaciers:
Glacial Till:
●Unsorted coarse and fine debris deposited by melting glaciers, with most
rock fragments angular to sub-angular in form.
Outwash Deposit:
●Glacio-fluvial deposits comprising rock debris small enough to be carried
by meltwater streams.
●Rock fragments have rounded edges.●Unlike till deposits, outwash deposits are roughly stratified and assorted.
Moraines:
●Long ridges of glacial till deposits.●Terminal Moraines: Long ridges of debris deposited at the end (toe) of
glaciers.
●Lateral Moraines: May join a terminal moraine, forming a
horseshoe-shaped ridge. Origin

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
Bars, Barriers, and Spits:
●An offshore ridge of sand and shingle lying parallel to the coast in the
off-shore zone is called an offshore bar.
●If an offshore bar becomes exposed due to further sand deposition, it is
termed a barrier bar.
●These bars and barriers often form across the mouths of rivers or at bay
entrances.
●A barrier bar attached to the coast from one end is known as a spit.
●Barrier bars, spits, and bars at bay mouths gradually extend, reducing the
bay's opening to the sea, eventually transforming it into a lagoon.
●Lagoons can later be filled with sand brought by wind, developing into
broad, wide plains.
Winds:
●Winds are one of the dominant agents in hot deserts.●Heated desert floors warm the air above, creating upward movements and
turbulence, forming eddies, whirlwinds, updrafts, and downdrafts.
●Winds move across desert floors rapidly, creating turbulence around
obstructions.
●Wind actions include deflation, abrasion, and impact.●Deflation involves lifting and removal of dust and smaller particles from
rock surfaces, contributing to the formation of various desert landforms.
●Many desert features result from mass wasting and running water,
including sheet floods.
●Desert rocks, exposed to mechanical and chemical weathering due to
temperature changes, decay faster, aided by torrential rains that remove
weathered materials easily.
●Debris in deserts is moved not only by winds but also by rain and sheet
wash.
●Wind predominantly facilitates mass erosion thr

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
●During periods of sufficient water, playas may be covered by shallow
water bodies, but these evaporate quickly, leaving behind salt deposits.
●Playas may contain alkali flats, flat plains covered by salt deposits.
Deflation Hollows and Caves:
●Weathered mantle or bare soil is blown away by persistent wind currents,
forming shallow depressions known as deflation hollows.
●Wind-driven sand causes impact and abrasion on rock surfaces, creating
small pits or cavities known as blowouts.
●Some blowouts deepen and widen over time, forming larger depressions
called caves.
Mushroom, Table, and Pedestal Rocks:
●In desert environments, some rock outcrops are susceptible to wind
deflation and abrasion.
●Over time, these rocks may be worn down, leaving behind remnants of
resistant rocks shaped like mushrooms, with a slender stalk and a broad,
rounded cap.
●Similarly, some remnants may have a top surface broad like a tabletop
and stand out like pedestals.
Depositional Landforms in Deserts:
●Wind acts as a sorting agent in deserts, sorting grains of different sizes as
it transports them.
●When wind velocity decreases, the sorted grains begin to settle, leading to
the formation of well-sorted depositional landforms.
Sand Dunes:
●Dry, hot deserts are ideal environments for sand dune formation, often
initiated by obstacles.
●Various types of dunes can form, depending on factors such as wind
direction, sand supply, and the presence of vegetation:
○Barchans: Crescent-shaped dunes with the point

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
Continuation of Structure of the Atmosphere:
●Stratosphere:
○Located above the tropopause, extending to 50 km altitude.
○Houses the ozone layer, absorbing ultraviolet radiation and
shielding life from harmful energy.
●Mesosphere:
○Lies above the stratosphere, extending up to 80 km altitude.○Temperature decreases with altitude, reaching as low as -100°C at
80 km.
○Terminates at the mesopause.
●Ionosphere:
○Positioned between 80 and 400 km above the mesopause.○Contains electrically charged ions, reflecting radio waves
transmitted from earth.
○Temperature increases with altitude.
●Exosphere:
○Uppermost layer above the thermosphere.○Limited understanding due to rarity of contents, gradually merging
with outer space.
Influential Elements of the Atmosphere:
●Key factors influencing human life includetemperature, pressure, winds,
humidity, clouds, and precipitation.
Chapter 9: SOLAR RADIATION, HEAT BALANCE AND
TEMPERATURE
Solar Radiation:
The Earth's Surface and Insolation:
●The Earth's surface primarily receives energy in short wavelengths.
●Incoming solar radiation, or insolation, constitutes the energy received by
the Earth.
●Due to the Earth's geoid shape, sunlight falls obliquely at the top of the
atmosphere, with the Earth intercepting a small portion of solar energy.
●Average insolation at the top of the atmosphere is 1.94 calories per sq. cm
per minute.
●Solar output varies slightly throughout the year due to Earth-Sun distance
variations.
●Aphelion occurs when Earth is fart

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
●Perihelion happens on January 3rd, when Earth is closest to the Sun (147
million km), resulting in slightly higher insolation compared to July 4th.
●This variation in solar output is overshadowed by factors like land-sea
distribution and atmospheric circulation, with minimal impact on daily
weather.
Variation of Insolation at Earth's Surface:
●Insolation varies throughout the day, season, and year due to multiple
factors.
●Factors influencing insolation include:
○Earth's rotation on its axis.
○Angle of inclination of the Sun's rays.○Duration of daylight.○Atmospheric transparency.○Land configuration, particularly aspect.
Influence of Earth's Axis and Latitude:
●Earth's axis tilt of 66½o significantly impacts insolation distribution across
latitudes.
●Latitude determines the angle of inclination of solar rays, with higher
latitudes receiving slanted rays.
●Slanted rays cover less area, resulting in energy distribution and reduced
net energy per unit area.
●Slanted rays also traverse a greater atmospheric depth, leading to
increased absorption, scattering, and diffusion of energy.
Passage of Solar Radiation through the Atmosphere:Atmospheric Transmission:
●The atmosphere is primarily transparent to shortwave solar radiation.●Within the troposphere, water vapor, ozone, and other gases absorb
near-infrared radiation.
●Small suspended particles in the troposphere scatter visible light, both
towards space and the Earth's surface, contributing to the sky's color.
●The red hues of su

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
Variation in the Net Heat Budget at the Earth's Surface:
Regional Disparities in Radiation Balance:
●Certain regions experience a surplus in radiation balance, while others
face a deficit.
●Surplus radiation balance occurs roughly between 40 degrees north and
south latitudes, while regions near the poles have a deficit.
Redistribution of Heat Energy:
●Surplus heat energy from the tropics is redistributed towards the poles.●This redistribution prevents the tropics from excessively heating due to
accumulated excess heat.
●Similarly, it prevents high latitudes from becoming permanently frozen due
to excess deficit.
Temperature:Definition and Measurement:
●Heat generated from the interaction of insolation with the atmosphere and
Earth's surface is quantified as temperature.
●Temperature reflects the molecular movement of substance particles,
measured in degrees of hot or cold.
Factors Controlling Temperature Distribution:Latitude:
●Temperature correlates with the amount of insolation received, varying
with latitude.
Altitude:
●Near-sea level areas generally exhibit higher temperatures due to indirect
heating from terrestrial radiation. Conversely, temperature decreases with
altitude.
●Normal lapse rate describes the rate of temperature decrease with height.
Distance from the Sea:
●Land heats and cools rapidly compared to the ocean, which heats and
cools slowly.
●Proximity to the sea moderates temperature through sea and land
breezes.
Air Masses and Oceanic Currents:
●Movement of

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
Definition and Occurrence:
●Normally, temperature decreases with elevation, known as the normal
lapse rate. In some cases, this situation is reversed, termed as
temperature inversion. Inversions are typically short-lived.
●Ideal conditions for inversion occur on long winter nights with clear skies
and still air, where Earth cools faster than the air above. Inversions arecommon over polar areas throughout the year.
Effects of Inversion:
●Surface inversion enhances stability in lower atmospheric layers.
●Smoke and dust particles accumulate beneath the inversion layer,
spreading horizontally and filling lower atmospheric strata.
●Dense morning fogs are frequent, especially during winter.
In Hilly or Mountainous Areas:
●Inversion occurs due to air drainage.●Cold air generated in hills and mountains during the night descends under
gravity's influence.
●Dense cold air flows down slopes, accumulating in valleys with warm air
above, known as air drainage, which protects plants from frost damage.
Interesting Points:Plank's Law:
●States that hotter bodies emit more energy, with shorter wavelengths.
Specific Heat:
●The amount of energy required to raise the temperature of one gram of
substance by one degree Celsius.
Chapter 10: ATMOSPHERIC CIRCULATION AND WEATHER
SYSTEMS
Atmospheric Pressure:
●Air expands when heated and contracts when cooled, leading to
variations in atmospheric pressure.
●Atmospheric pressure is the weight of a column of air per unit area from
mean sea level to the to

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
Pressure Gradient Force:
●Differences in atmospheric pressure generate a force.
●The rate of pressure change concerning distance is the pressure gradient.●Strong pressure gradients occur when isobars are close together, and
weak gradients occur when they are far apart.
Frictional Force:
●Influences wind speed.●Most significant at the surface, extending up to 1-3 km elevation.●Minimal friction over the sea surface.
Coriolis Force:
●Earth's rotation affects wind direction.●Named after French physicist who described it in 1844.●Deflects wind to the right in the Northern Hemisphere and to the left in the
Southern Hemisphere, with greater deflection at higher wind velocities.

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
●Directly proportional to latitude angle, maximum at poles, absent at the
equator.
●Acts perpendicular to pressure gradient force, which is perpendicular to
isobars.
●Higher pressure gradient force results in faster wind velocity and greater
deflection.
●In low-pressure areas, wind circulates around them due to perpendicular
forces.
●Zero at the equator, wind blows perpendicular to isobars, preventing
intensification of low pressure and formation of tropical cyclones.
Pressure and Wind:
●Wind velocity and direction result from wind-generating forces.
●Upper atmosphere winds (2-3 km above) controlled by pressure gradient
force and Coriolis force, free from friction.
●In conditions of straight isobars and no friction, pressure gradient force
balances Coriolis force, resulting in geostrophic wind parallel to isobars.
●Wind circulation around low pressure is cyclonic, around high pressure is
anticyclonic, with opposite directions.
●Surface wind circulation often related to higher-level wind circulation.●Convergence and rising air over low-pressure areas, subsidence and
divergence over high-pressure areas.
●Other factors like eddies, convection currents, orographic uplift, and uplift
along fronts contribute to air rising, crucial for cloud and precipitation
formation.
General Circulation of the Atmosphere:
●Pattern of planetary winds' movement.
●Influenced by:
○Latitudinal variation of atmospheric heating.○Emergence of pressure belts.○Migration of belts following the sun's apparen

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
Ferrell Cell:
●Middle latitudes have sinking cold air from poles and rising warm air from
subtropical high.
●Surface winds called westerlies; cell known as Ferrell cell.
Polar Cell:
●At polar latitudes, cold dense air subsides near poles and blows towards
middle latitudes as polar easterlies.
●Cell referred to as Polar cell.
Impact and Maintenance:
●Transfer of heat energy from lower to higher latitudes maintains general
circulation.
●General circulation affects oceans:
○Large-scale winds initiate slow-moving ocean currents.
○Oceans contribute energy and water vapor to the air.
Seasonal Winds:
●Wind circulation pattern modified in different seasons due to shifting
regions of maximum heating, pressure, and wind belts.
●Most pronounced effect seen in monsoons, especially over Southeast
Asia.
Local Winds:
●Differences in heating and cooling of Earth's surfaces create various
common, local, or regional winds.
●Land and Sea Breezes:
○During the day, land heats up faster than sea, creating low-pressure
area over land and high-pressure area over sea. Wind blows from
sea to land.
○At night, land loses heat faster and becomes cooler than sea.
Pressure gradient is from land to sea, resulting in land breeze.
Mountain and Valley Winds:
●During the day in mountainous regions, heated slopes cause air to move
upslope, creating a gap filled by air from the valley, known as valley
breeze.
●At night, cooled slopes result in dense air descending into the valley,
creating mountain wind.
●Cool ai

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
Air Masses:
●Air remaining over homogenous area for extended time acquires area's
characteristics.
●Homogenous regions like oceans or plains form source regions for air
masses.
●Air masses have distinctive temperature and humidity characteristics.
●Source regions classify air masses:
○Warm tropical and subtropical oceans.○Subtropical hot deserts.○Relatively cold high latitude oceans.○Very cold snow-covered continents in high latitudes.○Permanently ice-covered continents in Arctic and Antarctica.
●Types of air masses: (i) Maritime tropical (mT); (ii) Continental tropical
(cT); (iii) Maritime polar (mP); (iv) Continental polar (cP); (v) Continental
arctic (cA).
●Tropical air masses are warm, polar air masses are cold.

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
Fronts:
●Boundary zone between two different air masses.
●Formation process known as frontogenesis.●Four types of fronts:
○Cold Front: Cold air moves towards warm air mass, forming cold
front.
○Warm Front: Warm air mass moves towards cold air mass, forming
warm front.
○Stationary Front: Front remains stationary.○Occluded Front: Air mass fully lifted above land surface.
●Fronts occur in middle latitudes, characterized by steep temperature and
pressure gradients.
●Bring abrupt temperature changes, cause air to rise, form clouds, and
induce precipitation.

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
Extra-Tropical Cyclones:
●Systems developing in mid and high latitudes beyond tropics.
●Form along polar front.●Formation:
○Initially, front is stationary.○In Northern Hemisphere, warm air from south and cold air from
north of front.
○Pressure drop along front leads to warm air moving north and cold
air moving south, initiating anticlockwise cyclonic circulation.
○Cyclonic circulation leads to well-developed extra-tropical cyclone
with warm and cold fronts.
●Warm air glides over cold air, resulting in cloud sequence ahead of warm
front and precipitation.
●Cold front approaches warm air from behind, pushing warm air up, leading
to cumulus cloud development along cold front.
●Cold front moves faster, overtaking warm front.●Warm air completely lifted, front occluded, and cyclone dissipates.●Wind circulation processes at surface and aloft are closely linked.
Tropical Cyclones:
●Originate and intensify over warm tropical oceans.●Violent storms originating over tropical oceans and moving towards
coastal areas, causing large-scale destruction with violent winds, heavy
rainfall, and storm surges.
●Known as:
○Cyclones in the Indian Ocean.
○Hurricanes in the Atlantic.○Typhoons in the Western Pacific and South China Sea.○Willy-willies in Western Australia.
Favorable Conditions for Tropical Cyclones:
●Large sea surface with temperature higher than 27°C.
●Presence of the Coriolis force.●Small variations in vertical wind speed.●Pre-existing weak low-pressure area or low-level cyclonic circ

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
○Possess a flat base.
3.Stratus:
○Layered clouds covering large portions of the sky.
○Formed due to heat loss or mixing of air masses with different
temperatures.
4.Nimbus:
○Black or dark grey in color.○Form at middle levels or very near to the earth's surface.○Extremely dense and opaque to sunlight.○Sometimes appear to touch the ground.○Shapeless masses of thick vapor.
●Combination of Basic Types:
○High Clouds:Cirrus, cirrostratus, cirrocumulus.○Middle Clouds:Altostratus and altocumulus.○Low Clouds:Stratocumulus and nimbostratus.○Clouds with extensive vertical development: Cumulus and
cumulonimbus.
Key Points:
1. Clouds are formed by the condensation of water vapor at considerable
heights.
2. Four primary types of clouds are cirrus, cumulus, stratus, and nimbus,
distinguished by their altitude, appearance, and color.
3. Cirrus clouds are thin and feathery, cumulus clouds resemble cotton wool,
stratus clouds are layered, and nimbus clouds are dark and dense.
4. Cloud types can be further classified into high, middle, and low clouds
based on their altitude, with cumulus and cumulonimbus clouds showing
extensive vertical development.
Precipitation:
●Definition:
○Precipitation occurs when condensed particles in the atmosphere
grow in size until the air resistance can no longer hold them against
gravity, causing them to fall to the earth's surface.
●Types of Precipitation:
1.Rainfall:
○Precipitation in the form of water droplets.
2.Snowfall:
○Occurs when temperatures are below 0°

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
3. Cyclonic Rainfall:
○Caused by tropical and extra-tropical cyclones.
World Distribution of Rainfall:
●Variability:
○Different regions receive varying amounts of rainfall annually and
seasonally.
○Rainfall diminishes from the equator towards the poles.
●Coastal Influence:
○Coastal areas receive more rainfall compared to inland regions.
○Oceans yield more rainfall than landmasses due to their vast water
bodies.
●Directional Patterns:
○Heavier rainfall occurs on eastern coasts between latitudes 35° and
40° N and S, decreasing towards the west.
○Between latitudes 45° and 65° N and S, westerlies cause rainfall on
western margins of continents, diminishing towards the east.
●Mountainous Areas:
○Rainfall is greater on windward sides of mountains parallel to the
coast, decreasing on leeward sides.
●Major Precipitation Regimes:
○Heavy Rainfall (over 200 cm/year):
■Equatorial belt and windward slopes of western coastal
mountains in cool temperate zones.
○Moderate Rainfall (200 cm/year):
■Interior continental areas and coastal regions.
○Low Rainfall (50 - 100 cm/year):
■Central tropical lands and eastern/interior parts of temperate
lands.
○Very Low Rainfall (less than 50 cm/year):
■Rain shadow zones of continental interiors and high latitudes.
●Uniform Distribution:
○Equatorial belt and western parts of cool temperate regions
experience even rainfall throughout the year.
Interesting Points:
●Dew Point:
○Air saturated with moisture at a given temperature is termed as
having reached its

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
Major Climatic Groups:
1.Humid Climates:
○Designated by capital letters A, C, D, and E.
○Based on temperature.
2.Dry Climates:
○Designated by capital letter B.○Based on precipitation.
Climatic Types:
●Subdivided within climatic groups, indicated by small letters, based on
seasonality and temperature characteristics.
●Seasons of Dryness:
○f:No dry season.○m:Monsoon climate.○w:Winter dry season.○s:Summer dry season.
●Degree of Temperature Severity:
○a, b, c, d:Indicates severity.
●Subdivision of Dry Climates (B):
○S:Steppe or semi-arid.○W:Deserts.
Group A: Tropical Humid ClimatesCharacteristics:
●Exist between the Tropic of Cancer and Tropic of Capricorn.●Climate is hot and humid due to the sun being overhead throughout the
year and the presence of the Inter Tropical Convergence Zone (ITCZ).
●Annual temperature range is minimal, with high annual rainfall.
Tropical Wet Climate (Af):
●Regions:
○Found near the equator, major areas include the Amazon Basin in
South America, western equatorial Africa, and the islands of the
East Indies.
●Rainfall:
○Significant rainfall occurs every month, mainly as afternoon thunder
showers.
●Temperature:
○Uniformly high temperatures with negligible annual temperature
range.
○Maximum temperature around 30°C, minimum around 20°C.
●Vegetation and Biodiversity:
○Dense tropical evergreen forests with abundant biodiversity.
Tropical Monsoon Climate (Am):
●Regions:
○Found over the Indian subcontinent, northeastern South America,
and northern Australia.
●R

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
Climate Change:
The Earth has experienced numerous climate variations throughout its history,
with evidence suggesting that climate change is a natural and ongoing process:
●Geological records document changes between glacial and interglacial
periods.
●Geomorphological features, especially in high altitudes and latitudes,
show evidence of glacier advances and retreats.
●Sediment deposits in glacial lakes indicate warm and cold periods.
●Tree rings provide insights into wet and dry periods.●The Rajasthan desert experienced a wet and cool climate around 8,000
B.C.
Stages of Climate Change:
●Approximately 500-300 million years ago, the Earth was warm during the
Cambrian, Ordovician, and Silurian periods.
●During the Pleistocene epoch, there were glacial and interglacial periods,
with the last major peak glacial period occurring about 18,000 years ago.
●The present interglacial period began 10,000 years ago.
Causes of Climate Change:These causes can be categorized as astronomical
and terrestrial.
Astronomical Causes:
●Changes in solar output associated with sunspot activities.
○Sunspots are dark, cooler patches on the sun that vary cyclically.○Increase in sunspot numbers is associated with cooler, wetter
weather and increased storminess.
○Decrease in sunspot numbers is associated with warmer, drier
conditions.
●Milankovitch Oscillations:
○Cycles in variations of Earth's orbital characteristics around the sun,
wobbling of the Earth, and changes in Earth's axial tilt.
○These altera

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
International Efforts to Reduce GHGs:
●Kyoto Protocol (1997): Effective from 2005, binds 35 industrialized
countries to reduce emissions by 5% below 1990 levels by 2012.
Greenhouse Effect:
●Derived from the analogy to a greenhouse used in cold areas for heat
preservation.
●A greenhouse is made of glass:
○Transparent to incoming shortwave solar radiation.
○Opaque to outgoing longwave radiation.
●Glass allows more radiation in and prevents longwave radiation from
escaping.
●Result: Temperature inside the greenhouse is warmer than outside.
The Dust Bowl:
●Refers to the Great Plains of the United States.●Characterized by severe dust storms during the 1930s.●Caused by prolonged drought and poor land management practices.●Resulted in significant agricultural and ecological damage, leading to
economic hardship for many.
Chapter 13: WATER
Hydrological Cycle
The hydrological cycle denotes the continuous movement of water within the
earth's hydrosphere in its various states: liquid, solid, and gaseous. Itencompasses the exchange of water among the oceans, atmosphere, landsurface, subsurface, and organisms.
Key Points:
1. Cyclic Nature of Water:
○Water is a cyclic resource, capable of being utilized and re-utilized.
2. Ocean-Land Exchange:
○Water undergoes a cycle transitioning from ocean to land and vice
versa.
3. Description of the Hydrological Cycle:
○It describes the movement of water across, within, and above the
earth's surface.

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
4. Uneven Distribution:
○Water distribution on earth is highly uneven, with some regions
having abundant water while others face scarcity.
5. Water Reservoirs:
○Approximately 71% of the earth's water is contained within the
oceans.
○The remaining portion exists as freshwater in glaciers, icecaps,
groundwater sources, lakes, soil moisture, atmosphere, streams,
and within living organisms.
6. Evaporation and Runoff:
○Nearly 59% of water falling on land returns to the atmosphere
through evaporation, occurring predominantly over oceans.
○The remaining portion either runs off on the surface, infiltrates into
the ground, or contributes to glacier formation.
Relief of the Ocean Floor

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
The oceanic topography is characterized by the configuration of the earth's outer
layer, primarily consisting of deep depressions where oceans are situated.
Key Points:
1. Natural Integration of Oceans:
○Unlike continents, oceans blend seamlessly into one another,
making it challenging to delineate their boundaries.
2. Division of Oceans:
○Geographers have classified the oceanic realm into five distinct
bodies of water: the Pacific, Atlantic, Indian, Southern, and Arctic
Oceans.
3. Inlets and Coastal Features:
○Seas, bays, gulfs, and other coastal indentations are integral parts
of these vast oceans, enhancing their diversity.
4. Depth of Ocean Floor:
○A significant portion of the ocean floor lies submerged between
depths of 3 to 6 kilometers beneath the sea level.
5. Complexity of Ocean Floor:
○The submerged landmass beneath the ocean surface, known as the
ocean floor, exhibits intricate and diverse features akin to thosefound on terrestrial landscapes.
6. Geological Features:
○Ocean floors boast rugged terrains, comprising the world's largest
mountain ranges, deepest trenches, and expansive plains.
○These features are primarily shaped by tectonic activities, volcanic
eruptions, and depositional processes, similar to those influencingcontinental landforms.
Division of the Ocean Floors
The ocean floors are categorized into four primary divisions: the Continental
Shelf, the Continental Slope, the Deep-Sea Plain, and the Oceanic Deeps.
Additionally, various major and minor reli

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
■Temperatures range between 20° and 25°C.
■Present year-round in tropical regions but develops only
during summer in mid-latitudes.
○Second Layer (Thermocline Layer):
■Lies below the first layer, characterized by a rapid
temperature decrease with depth.
■Thermocline thickness ranges from 500 to 1,000 meters.
○Third Layer:
■Very cold and extends to the deep ocean floor.■In Arctic and Antarctic regions, surface water temperatures
are close to 0°C, resulting in minimal temperature change
with depth.
■Only one layer of cold water exists, extending from the
surface to the deep ocean floor.
Horizontal Distribution of Temperature:
The temperature of ocean surface waters varies horizontally, influenced by
latitude and geographic location.
●Equatorial Regions to Poles:
○Average surface water temperature is around 27°C, gradually
decreasing from the equator towards the poles.
○The rate of temperature decrease with increasing latitude is
approximately 0.5°C per latitude.
○At 20° latitudes, the average temperature is about 22°C, while at
40° latitudes, it decreases to around 14°C, and near the poles, itreaches 0°C.
●Hemispheric Variation:
○Oceans in the northern hemisphere generally have higher
temperatures compared to those in the southern hemisphere.
○The highest ocean temperatures are typically found slightly north of
the equator.
○Average annual temperatures for the northern and southern
hemispheres are approximately 19°C and 16°C, respectively,
influenced by the unequal distribution

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
Salinity levels in oceans vary across different regions, influenced by
geographical factors, climate, and water circulation patterns.
●Normal Ocean Salinity:
○Ranges between 33 to 37 parts per thousand (PPT) in open ocean
areas.
○Peaks at around 41 PPT in landlocked seas like the Red Sea.
○In estuaries and the Arctic, salinity fluctuates seasonally between 0
and 35 PPT.
●Extreme Salinity Conditions:
○In hot and dry regions with high evaporation rates, salinity can
reach up to 70 PPT.
●Regional Variations:
○Pacific Ocean: Salinity variations attributed to its shape and vast
size.
○Atlantic Ocean: Average salinity around 36 PPT, with higher levels
observed between latitudes 15° and 20°, gradually decreasing
northward.
○Baltic Sea: Low salinity due to significant freshwater influx from
rivers.
○Mediterranean Sea: Higher salinity due to intense evaporation.
○Black Sea: Low salinity due to substantial freshwater input from
rivers.
○Indian Ocean: Average salinity around 35 PPT, with variations due
to river inflow and evaporation rates.
○Bay of Bengal: Lower salinity due to river influx, while Arabian Sea
exhibits higher salinity owing to low freshwater input and high
evaporation.
Vertical Distribution of Salinity:
Salinity undergoes changes with depth, but the pattern varies depending on the
location of the sea.
●Surface vs. Depth:
○Surface salinity influenced by factors such as ice formation,
evaporation, and freshwater input from rivers.
○Salinity at depth remains relatively cons

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
●Desilting and Water Quality:Tides help in desilting sediments and
removing polluted water from river estuaries.
●Energy Generation:Tides are utilized to generate electrical power in
various countries, including Canada, France, Russia, and China. In India,
a 3 MW tidal power project atDurgaduani in the Sunderbans of WestBengal is underway.
Ocean Currents:
●Description:Ocean currents are akin to river flows within oceans,
representing a regular volume of water following a definite path anddirection.
●Influencing Forces:
○Primary Forces:
■Heating by Solar Energy:Solar energy causes water to
expand, creating a slight gradient where water tends to flowdown the slope.
■Wind:Surface winds push water, and friction between wind
and the water surface affects its movement.
■Gravity:Pulls water down and creates gradient variations.
■Coriolis Force:Causes water to move to the right in the
Northern Hemisphere and to the left in the Southern
Hemisphere, resulting in the accumulation of water in certainlocations. These accumulations and the flow around themform gyres, producing large circular currents in all oceanbasins.
○Secondary Forces:
■Density Differences:Differences in water density, influenced
by factors such as salinity and temperature, affect the verticalmobility of ocean currents.
■Salinity:Water with higher salinity is denser than water
with lower salinity, and cold water is denser than warmwater.
■Temperature:Denser, colder water tends to sink, while
relatively lighter, warmer w

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
●Heat Transport:
○Oceanic circulation plays a vital role in transporting heat from one
latitude belt to another, similar to the heat transport by the general
circulation of the atmosphere.
●Polar and Equatorial Movements:
○Cold waters from the Arctic and Antarctic circles move towards
warmer tropical and equatorial regions.
○Conversely, warm waters from lower latitudes move polewards.
Effects of Ocean Currents:
Ocean currents exert various direct and indirect influences on human activities,
affecting climates, ecosystems, and economic sectors such as fishing. Here aresome notable effects:
●Cool Waters on West Coasts (Tropical and Subtropical Latitudes):
○West coasts of continents in tropical and subtropical latitudes,
excluding equatorial regions, are bordered by cool waters.
○These areas typically experience relatively low average
temperatures with narrow diurnal and annual temperature ranges.
○Fog may occur, and the regions are generally arid.
●Warm Waters on West Coasts (Middle and Higher Latitudes):
○West coasts of continents in middle and higher latitudes are
bordered by warm waters, resulting in a distinct marine climate.
○These areas exhibit cool summers and relatively mild winters with
narrow annual temperature ranges.
●Warm Currents along East Coasts (Tropical and Subtropical
Latitudes):
○Warm currents flow parallel to the east coasts of continents in
tropical and subtropical latitudes, leading to warm and rainyclimates.
○These areas lie on the western margins of sub

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
●Photosynthesis:During photosynthesis, carbon dioxide is converted into
organic compounds, while oxygen is released as a byproduct.
●Solar Insolation:Only a small fraction (0.1 percent) of solar insolation
reaching the Earth's surface is fixed through photosynthesis. A significant
portion is utilized for plant respiration, with the remaining stored ortransferred within plants.
●Systemic Flows:Organisms survive through systemic flows of energy,
water, and nutrients, which vary across different regions and seasons dueto local factors.
●Chemical Balance:The balance of chemical components in the
atmosphere and hydrosphere is maintained through cyclic passagethrough plant and animal tissues, followed by release throughdecomposition.
●Biogeochemical Cycles:
○Definition:Cyclic movements of chemical elements between
organisms and the environment are termed biogeochemical cycles.
○Types:
■Gaseous Cycle:Nutrient reservoirs primarily include the
atmosphere and the ocean.
■Sedimentary Cycle:Main reservoirs consist of soil,
sedimentary rocks, and other earth crust components.
The Water Cycle:
●Definition:All living organisms, the atmosphere, and the lithosphere
maintain a circulation of water in solid, liquid, or gaseous forms, referredto as the water or hydrologic cycle.
The Carbon Cycle:
●Carbon in Living Organisms:
○Carbon is a fundamental element in all living organisms and
constitutes the basic building blocks of organic compounds.
●Process Overview:
○Photosynthesis:The carbon cycle

chunks.source: ISSF Geography NCERT Class VI-XI Summary.pdf
chunks.text:
●Nitrogen Fixation:
○Soil microorganisms, along with plant roots, primarily fix
atmospheric nitrogen into an available form.
○Other sources of nitrogen fixation include lightning, cosmic
radiation, and certain marine animals in oceans.
●Assimilation by Plants:
○Once nitrogen is fixed into an available form, green plants can
assimilate it for growth and development.
●Consumption by Herbivores:
○Herbivorous animals consume nitrogen-containing plants, thereby
incorporating nitrogen into their own bodies.
●Decomposition and Recycling:
○Dead plants and animals, along with nitrogenous waste excretion,
are decomposed by soil bacteria.
○Bacteria convert nitrogenous compounds into nitrites and nitrates,
which can be used again by green plants.
●Denitrification:
○Certain bacteria facilitate denitrification, converting nitrates back
into free nitrogen, thus completing the nitrogen cycle.
Key Processes:
●Fixation:Conversion of atmospheric nitrogen into an available form by
soil microorganisms and associated plant roots.
●Assimilation:Incorporation of fixed nitrogen by green plants for growth
and development.
●Consumption:Herbivorous animals consume nitrogen-containing plants,
transferring nitrogen within the food chain.
●Decomposition:Soil bacteria decompose dead organic matter, releasing
nitrogenous compounds back into the soil.
●Recycling:Nitrogenous compounds are converted into usable forms by
bacteria, facilitating the continuous recycling of nitrogen within
ecosystems.

●

Dibru -Saikhowa  is a National Park as well as a Biosphere Reserve situated in the south bank of the river

Maguri Motapung wetland is a part of the Reserve.

National Centre for Polar and Ocean Research (NCPOR) was established as an autonomous research and 
development institution of the Ministry of Eart h Sciences  in 1998.

realms.  
• It is the nodal agency for planning, promo tion, coordination and execution of the entire 
gamut of polar and Southern  Ocean scientific research in the country as well as for the 
associated logistics activities.  
Its major responsibilities include:  
• Management and upkeep of the Indian Antarctic Resea rch Bases “ MAITRI ” and 
“Bharati ”, and the Indian Arctic base “ HIMADRI ”.

Festival is being hosted by World Wide Fund for Nature -India (WWF) and the Bombay  Natural History 
Society (BNHS) . It is third edition of festival , this year in Delhi.  
The festival is an effort to sensitise city dwellers about these insects, which a re natural pest control

Fly Ash : 
Fly ash is produced during coal combustio n. 
In 1999, Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (E FOCC) issued notification in 
1999 to ensure  100% utilisation of fly ash  under EPA, 1986.  
In 2015, GoI fixed December 31, 2017 as revised deadline to achieve 100% utilisation.  
 
Fly Ash usage  
Manufacture of cement, concrete, concrete products, cellular concr ete products, bricks/blocks/ 
tiles etc . 
Also used in agriculture to increase soil fertility and water retention ability.

Huge amount of Carbon Dioxide released by Himalayan Geothermal Springs : 
The carbon dioxide released from the thermal spring s are sourced from metamorphic decarbonation of  
carbonate rocks, magmatism and oxidation of graphite. These rocks are present  in the deep Himalayan 
core.  
The carbon outflux from the interior of the earth through volcanic eruptions, geotherm al systems and 
fault zones contribute to global carbon cycle.

Dimensions Length ~2,400 km from the Potwar Plateau (Pakistan) in the west to the Brahmaputra 
Valley in the east.
Elevation 900–1,100 meters.
ContinuityNorthwest to Gandak River Mostly continuous low hills (~1,200 km).
Gandak to Teesta River Highly dissected and discontinuous due to rivers and streams.
Beyond Teesta River Gaps of 80–90 km; hills become highly fragmented or non-existent due to heavy rainfall and numerous streams.
Forests

Process of Dun Formation
Himalayan rivers brought 
down conglomerates.Deposits obstructed river flow, creating temporary lakes.Lakes filled with sediments and drained over time, leaving flat valleys (duns).

Nepal HimalayasShields India from cold Siberian winds, shaping monsoon patterns.
Source of major rivers (Ganga, Indus, Brahmaputra), sustaining millions.

Acts as a strategic barrier against invasions.
Boosts tourism, pilgrimage (Kedarnath, Amarnath), and cultural heritage.Significance of Himalayas

Lies between Peninsular and Himalayan regions; youthful and tectonically active.
Largest alluvial tract globally, spanning 3,200 km (2,400 km in India).

Thar DesertCold 
DesertLies northwest of the Aravalli hills Thar Desert
Characterized by undulating terrain with longitudinal 
dunes and barchans.
Climate and VegetationExtremely arid climate with annual rainfall below 150 mm.
Sparse vegetation cover due to low precipitation.
Historical Significance Believed to have been under the sea during the Mesozoic era.
Evidence includes Wood Fossils Park at Aakal:  Fossils estimated to be 180 million years old.
Marine deposits:  Found near Brahmsar, Jaisalmer.
Geological Features
Underlying rock structure extends from the Peninsular plateau.
Surface features shaped by physical weathering and wind action.
Distinct landforms includes
Mushroom rocks
Shifting sand dunesOases (mostly in the southern part)Drainage and Orientation
Northern slope:
Towards Sindh.Southern slope:Towards the Rann of Kachchh.
Rivers are mostly ephemeral, with the Luni River being significant in the southern desert.
Streams often disappear, joining lakes or playas, which exhibit brackish water.
High evaporation and minimal precipitation result in acute water deficit in the region.Thar Desert

Eastern Coastal Plains
Extent Subarnarekha River (WB-Odisha border) to Kanniyakumari.
Width Broader than western plains (~120 km average).
Type Emergent coast, with a continental shelf extending up to 500 km.
Deltas Formed by rivers Mahanadi, Godavari, Krishna, and Cauvery.
Harbours Limited natural harbours.Utkal Plain
Coastal Odisha, includes the

Andhra Plain
South of Utkal Plain, up to Pulicat Lake.
Features Merged deltas of Godavari and Krishna rivers.
Inland shift of Kolleru Lake due to delta movement.
Harbours: Vishakhapatnam, Machilipatnam.Tamil Nadu Plain
From Pulicat Lake to Kanniyakumari.
Features Cauvery Delta (widens to 130 km), fertile and well-irrigated.
Referred to as Coromandel Coast or Payan Ghat.
