There are two major groups of islands in India
Andaman and Nicobar Islands
Continuation of the Arakan Yoma range (extension of Purvanchal Hills in Myanmar).
Formed due to the collision between the Indian Plate and the Burma Minor Plate (part of the Eurasian Plate).
Divided into two groups: Separated by the 10° Channel.
Andaman Islands
Group of ~200 islands categori

Lakshadweep Islands
Coral islands, part of the Reunion Hotspot Volcanic Chain in the Arabian Sea.
Smallest Union Territory of India (uni-district UT).
Major Islands Kavaratti: Capital; located 360 km off Kerala’s coast.
Agatti: Houses the only airport.
Andrott: Largest island (4.90 sq km).
Minicoy: Southernmost (4.80 sq km; second largest
Aminidivi: Northernmost group of islands."Lakshadweep" translates to "hundred thousand islands" in Malayalam/Sanskrit
Key Features Flat topography with no significant hills, streams, or valleys.
Most islands have elevations <5 m, making them highly vulnerable 
to rising sea levels.
Storm beaches consist of pebbles, shingles, cobbles, and boulders.
Important Channels11° Channel: Separates Aminidivi Islands (north) from Cannannore Islands (south).
9° Channel: Separates main Lakshadweep group (north) from Minicoy (south).
8° Channel: Separates Minicoy (north) from Maldives (south).Coral islands, part of the Reunion Hotspot Volcanic Chain in the Arabian Sea.Coral islands, part of the Reunion Hotspot Volcanic Chain in the Arabian Sea.Coral islands, part of the Reunion Hotspot Volcanic Chain in the Arabian Sea.

New Moore Island
Small, uninhabited offshore sandbar in the Bay of Bengal
near the Ganges-Brahmaputra Delta.
Emerged after the Bhola cyclone (1970) periodically appears 
and disappears.
Claimed by India and Bangladesh due to potential oil and gas reserves.
Sovereignty linked to broader disputes over the Radcliffe Award and maritime boundaries.
Sagar Island
Located in the Ganges Delta, on the continental shelf of the Bay of Bengal.
Lies at the mouth of the Hooghly River, separated from the mainland by a river arm.

Riverine Islands
Mājuli Island
River island in the Brahmaputra River, Assam.
First island to be declared a district (2016).
Originally covered 1,300 km² (1790s), now 
reduced to 352 km ² (2014) due to erosion.
Formed by Brahmaputra, Kherkutia Xuti 
(anabranch), and Subansiri River.
Neo-Vaishnavite culture hub of Assam.
In UNESCO Tentative List (2004) for World Heritage Site nomination.Umananda Island
Smallest Inhabited River Island in the World
Situated in the Brahmaputra River, Assam.
Named after Uma (Parvati) and Ananda (Shiva's joy).
Mythological significance
Shiva burned Kamadeva with his third eye (Kalika Purana), hence also called Bhasmachal.
Also known as Peacock Island due to its shape.
Home to the endangered Golden Langur, found only in Western Assam and Bhutan.
Munroe Thuruthu
Inland island group at the confluence of Ashtamudi Lake and Kallada River in Kollam.
Named after Colonel John Munro of Travancore.
Known as the "Sinking Island of Kerala" due to rising water levels and erosion.
Comprises eight small islets covering 13.4 km².Bhavani Island
Located in Krishna River, upstream of Prakasam Barrage, Vijayawada.
Named after Goddess Bhavani (Kanaka Durga Temple) nearby.
Features include robotic dinosaur park, garden maze, mirror maze.Mājuli Islandājuli Islandā Umananda Island

1. Latitudinal Location
Regions south of the Tropic of Cancer Northern regions
Located in the tropics; high solar insolation.
Extreme summers and moderate winters.Warm temperate zone; less solar insolation.
Hot summers (due to loo winds) and cold winters (due to 
western disturbances).
2. Distance from the Sea
Coastal regions:  Equable/maritime climate.
Interior regions: Extreme/continental climate.
Monsoon winds first reach coastal areas, ensuring good rainfall.3. Rainfall Distribution
Rainfall decreases from east to west in the Indo-Gangetic plains due to moisture exhaustion.
Punjab and Haryana avoid desertification due to winter rains from western disturbances.
4. Himalayas
Acts as a climatic divide between India and Central Asia.
Prevents cold, dry winds in winter and blocks monsoon winds, causing orographic 
rainfall.
Diverts the Bay of Bengal 
monsoon winds into two branches:Towards north-west India.

5. Physiography
Determines annual rainfall based on relief: Windward side (e.g., Western Ghats): Heavy rainfall.

Rajasthan and Gujarat receive

Lack of orographic barriers.
Thar Desert Formation: No significant vertical wind movements or convection cells.

7 . Upper Air Circulation
Westerly Jet Stream (Winter)
Brings western disturbances to north-west India, 
causing winter rain and snowfall.Easterly Jet Stream (Summer)
Facilitates the onset of south-west monsoon due to heating of the Tibetan Plateau.
8. Western Disturbances
Frontal cyclones originating in the Mediterranean region.
Causes Winter rain in plains and snowfall in hills.
Cold waves in northern plains.
Importance:  Beneficial for rabi crops (e.g., wheat, 
barley).9. Tropical Cyclones
Originate in the Bay of Bengal and Arabian Sea.
Seasonal occurrenceLow-intensity cyclones during the south-west monsoon.
High-intensity cyclones during retreating monsoon (October–November).
Impact:  Significant weather changes along 
peninsular and eastern coasts.
10. El Nino
Reduced monsoon rainfall and Bay of Bengal cyclones.
Increased Arabian Sea cyclones.
Leads to droughts.La Nina
Enhanced monsoon rainfall and Bay of Bengal cyclones.
Suppressed Arabian Sea cyclones
Causes floods.ENSO
Ocean-atmosphere interaction during El Nino or La Nina events.

Surface Pressure and Winds
A high-pressure center develops north of the Himalayas in winter, influencing India's weather.
Dry continental winds flow from Central Asia to the Indian subcontinent.
These winds interact with trade winds over northwestern India, with the contact zone occasionally 
shifting eastward to the middle Ganga valley.
Jet Stream and Uppe

Surface Pressure and Winds:
Wind circulation reverses with the sun's northward shift.
By mid-July, the Inter-Tropical Convergence Zone (ITCZ) moves northward (20°–25°N).
The ITCZ attracts moist maritime tropical air from the southern hemisphere, leading to the southwest monsoon.
Jet Streams and Upper Air Circulation
Westerly jet streams withdraw from India as the ITCZ shifts northward.
An easterly jet stream flows over southern India in June, with speeds of up to 90 km/h.
By August, it shifts to 15°N and by September to 22°N.
Easterly Jet Stream and Tropical Cyclones
The easterly jet stream steers tropical depressions crucial for monsoon rainfall distribution.
The frequency, direction, and intensity of these depressions determine rainfall patterns during 
the southwest monsoon period.Surface Pressure and Winds:

Changing Monsoon Patterns in India (1982–2022)
Pan-India tehsil-level analysis of 
changing monsoon trends duringSouthwest monsoon (June–September)
Northeast monsoon (October–December)
Focus on last decade (2012–2022) using WMO & IMD-endorsed statistical indices.Study Scope
Key Findings
Extreme Rainfall Variability: 23% of districts faced both deficient & excessive rainfall years.
(e.g., New Delhi, Bengaluru, Jaipur, Kachchh)
Southwest Monsoon Trends (2012–2022)55% of tehsils saw a >10% increase in rainfallEspecially in Rajasthan, Gujarat, Maharashtra, and Tamil Nadu.
11% of tehsils saw a >10% decreaseIndo-Gangetic plains, Northeast, and the Himalayas experiencing reduced rainfall during critical sowing months (June–July).
64% of tehsils reported a rise in heavy rainfall daysNotably in Maharashtra, Tamil Nadu, Gujarat, and Karnataka.
Northeast Monsoon Trends (2012–2022)Increased rainfall in Tamil Nadu, Andhra Pradesh, and Telangana.
Unusual rainfall rise in Maharashtra, Goa, Odisha, and West Bengal
48% of tehsils saw a >10% rise in October rainfall Likely due to delayed

Tropical Evergreen and Semi-Evergreen Forests
Found in the Western Ghats (western slopes), Northeastern 
hills, Andaman & Nicobar Islands.
Exist in warm and humid areas with rainfall > 200 cm and temperature > 22°C.
Tropical Evergreen Forests
Well-stratified with shrubs, creepers, short trees, and tall trees (up to 60 m).
No definite leaf-shedding period, remain green throughout the year.
Common species:  Rosewood, Mahogany, Aini, Ebony.
Semi-Evergreen Forests
Found in areas with less rainfall than evergreen forests.
Mixture of evergreen and moist deciduous trees.
Understory climbers give them an evergreen character.
Common species:  White Cedar, Hollock, Kail.Impact of British Rule
Large-scale forest exploitation.
Oak forests in Garhwal & Kumaon replaced 
with Pine (Chir) for railway sleepers.

The Gulf Stream is a warm ocean 
current that originates in the Gulf of 
Mexico.
Starts in the Gulf of Mexico, moves 
along the Florida coast, up the U.S. 
East Coast, and then crosses the 
North Atlantic Ocean.Eventually 
becomes part of the North Atlantic 
Drift and influences Western Europe.
Fast-moving – one of the fastest 
ocean currents. Surface speeds can 
exceed 2.5 meters per second near 
Florida.
Part of the Atlantic Meridional 
Overturning Circulation (AMOC) — a 
global "conveyor belt" of ocean 
currents.
A weakening Gulf Stream can cause 
extreme weather , cooling in Europe, 
rising sea levels on the U.S. East Coast, 
and disruption of monsoons in Asia 
and Africa.Theme-Oceanography (Ocean Currents)

Some important points
•General lowering of earths surface is known as Denudation
•Denudation involves both weathering and erosion.
•Rocks are first weakened by weathering, these weathered rock materials 
are then transported by agents of erosion such as Wind, River, Glaciers etc
•Running water is single most important agent of denudation.
When tributaries join the main river obliquely the drainage pattern so 
evolved will be tree like or dendritic. It develops in region of: 
•flat terrain 
•uniform topography 
•same type of rocks having equal resistance. 
WHERE? --→-> GREAT PLAINS OF INDIA.
If the tributaries join main river at right angles it is 
known as TRELLISED DRAINAGE PATTERN. 
WHERE -→In regions of ANTICLINE ( Mountains) and 
SYNCLINE ( Valley)
If a rivers originate from central high point and radiate in 
different directions it is known as RADIAL DRAINAGE 
PATTERN.

Condition Required For 
Cloud Seeding
Right atmospheric conditions with 
plenty of clouds that are capable of 
rain is required for cloud seeding. 
We cannot go and seed the clear 
skies.
Clouds form from the condensation 
of invisible water vapour on nuclei 
from dust, pollen and salt from 
ocean spray. 
Cloud seeding increases the 
number of these available nuclei. 
Raindrops or ice crystals might not 
form without these added nuclei.
Effective cloud seeding is only 
possible in the presence of clouds 
that are capable of producing rain
Hail is large, layered ice particles, often spherical in shape, which are produced by thunderstorms having strong, tilted updrafts. 
Hailstorms form within a unusually unstable air mass, that is, an air mass in which the temperature falloff with height is much greater than 
normal. The unstable air is necessary to produce large updraft speeds --fast enough to keep a developing hailstone from falling to the 
ground. Some of these updrafts can reach 60 mph or more. 
In a hailstorm, small ice particles that form above the freezing level (which occurs in all thunderstorms) collect either rai n water or cloud 
water on them, forming a water shell that freezes. The tilted updraft and downdraft structure of the storm is important in or derfor 
hailstones to grow because they can be 'recycled' several times, until they either become too large for the updraft to carry them, or they 
get caught in a downdraft, and they finally reach the ground.
